proposals.title: Quantitative Correlations Between Fractal Dimensions of Fracture Surfaces and Time-Dependent Mechanical Properties in Coal and Shale
proposals.motivation: The creep behavior and long-term strength of geomaterials like coal and shale are critical for predicting wellbore stability, hydraulic fracturing effectiveness, and long-term CO2 storage integrity in carbon-neutral energy strategies. Pre-2026 research by Xie and others has separately studied microscopic time-dependent mechanics and fractal fracture surfaces, but a direct, quantitative link between these two established concepts is lacking, limiting predictive models.
proposals.hypothesis: The fractal dimension (D) of fracture surfaces in coal and shale, derived from microscopic analysis, is a robust predictor of key time-dependent mechanical properties, specifically the creep compliance and long-term uniaxial compressive strength.
proposals.method: We will first generate controlled fracture surfaces in coal and shale samples via three-point bending and Brazilian tests. The 3D topography of these surfaces will be characterized using laser scanning confocal microscopy and atomic force microscopy. Fractal dimensions will be calculated using the cube-counting and power spectral density algorithms on the digital elevation data. Concurrently, the mechanical properties of sister samples will be measured via nanoindentation creep tests and long-term uniaxial compression experiments. Finally, multivariate regression and machine learning models (e.g., Random Forest) will be trained to establish quantitative correlations between D and the measured strength and creep parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirical, quantitative framework linking fractal geometry to time-dependent mechanical performance in energy-related geomaterials. It will deliver predictive models that can estimate long-term material behavior from surface topology scans, offering a practical tool for assessing reservoir stability and CO2 storage site integrity.
proposals.novelty: While fractal analysis of fractures is common, its direct correlation with *time-dependent* mechanical properties (creep) derived from nanoindentation, bridging the gap between surface geometry and bulk time-sensitive performance, is a novel and non-obvious synthesis of Xie's prior research themes.

proposals.title: Three-Dimensional Fractal Analysis of Nano-Asperity Evolution: Uncovering Atomic-Level Wear and Polishing Mechanisms in Coal and Shale Minerals
proposals.motivation: Understanding wear at the atomic scale is critical for optimizing processes like hydraulic fracturing in coal and shale, where surface interactions dictate fluid flow and fracture complexity. Pre-2026, while fractal methods have been applied to macroscopic fracture networks, the evolution of surface roughness at the nanometer scale, particularly for carbonaceous and silicate minerals, remains poorly quantified. This gap limits our ability to predict material behavior during long-term creep and abrasive processes relevant to energy extraction and carbon storage.
proposals.hypothesis: The time-dependent wear and polishing of mineral nano-asperities under mechanical stress follow a 3D fractal scaling law, where the evolution of surface complexity can be characterized by a changing fractal dimension (Df) that correlates directly with atomic-scale removal mechanisms and material properties.
proposals.method: We will employ high-resolution nanoindentation and atomic force microscopy (AFM) to generate controlled wear on polished surfaces of coal and shale samples. The resulting 3D topographical data will be analyzed using a cube-counting algorithm to compute the 3D fractal dimension (Df) of surface asperities before and after wear. This will be complemented by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) to correlate fractal parameter changes with observed microscopic fracture and particle detachment mechanisms. The time-dependent data from nanoindentation creep tests will be used to model Df evolution as a function of loading history.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a quantitative, 3D fractal framework for describing atomic-to-nanoscale wear, directly linking surface evolution metrics to mechanical processes. It will provide novel insights into the long-term geomechanical behavior of energy reservoirs, informing models for fracture sustainability and seal integrity in carbon storage applications. The methodology will extend fractal analysis from descriptive morphology to a predictive tool for surface evolution.
proposals.novelty: The novel integration of 3D fractal analysis with nanoindentation-based wear testing at the atomic scale for geological materials is unprecedented. Moving beyond 2D profilometry or macroscopic fracture networks to quantify the 3D fractal evolution of nano-asperities under load represents a significant methodological advance in surface science.

proposals.title: Fractal Characterization of Worn Coal-Cutting Tool Surfaces and Its Correlation with Workpiece Fracture and Energy Consumption
proposals.motivation: In coal mining and mechanical processing, tool wear critically affects cutting efficiency, energy consumption, and the resulting fracture characteristics of the workpiece (e.g., coal or rock). Pre-2026 studies on shale and coal have established that fracture surfaces and particle size distributions exhibit fractal scaling, influencing gas flow and mechanical behavior. However, a systematic fractal analysis of the evolving topography of the grinding/cutting tools themselves is lacking, creating a knowledge gap in linking tool state directly to process outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: The fractal dimension of a worn tool's surface topography quantitatively correlates with the fractal dimension of the generated workpiece fracture surfaces and the specific energy consumption of the cutting process.
proposals.method: We will employ scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and white-light interferometry to obtain high-resolution 3D topographies of coal-cutting pick tools at varying stages of wear. The fractal dimension (D) of these surfaces will be calculated using the cube-counting algorithm and power-spectral density methods, established pre-2026 fractal analysis techniques. Concurrently, we will perform controlled cutting tests on coal samples, analyzing the resulting debris and fracture surfaces using digital image analysis to determine their fractal dimension. Statistical regression will link tool-surface D to workpiece-surface D and measured cutting forces/energy.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a quantitative, scale-independent descriptor for tool wear state, moving beyond traditional metrics like volume loss. It will provide a mechanistic link between tool condition and the fundamental fracture processes in the workpiece, offering a novel framework for predicting cutting efficiency and product characteristics in coal mining and similar mechanical excavation processes.
proposals.novelty: While fractal analysis is applied to geological fractures and particles, applying the same rigorous microscopic fractal methodology to characterize the *tool* surface as it wears and directly correlating it with process outputs is a novel transposition of the method, creating a unified fractal description of the tool-workpiece interaction system.

proposals.title: Re-evaluating the Mechanical Role of Thermally-Induced Cracks in Coal: A Coupled Nanoindentation and Fractal Analysis of Creep and Toughness
proposals.motivation: Conventional understanding posits that thermal cracking, a common process in coalbed methane recovery, geothermal stimulation, and underground coal gasification, degrades material strength and integrity. This perspective overlooks the potential for crack networks to create complex, load-bearing micro-architectures that may enhance fracture toughness and alter time-dependent (creep) behavior. A mechanistic understanding of this duality is critical for optimizing engineering processes like hydraulic fracturing and carbon sequestration in coal under thermal stresses.
proposals.hypothesis: Thermally-induced microcracks in coal not only reduce quasi-static strength but also create tortuous, interlocking fracture networks that increase macroscopic fracture toughness and modify the viscoelastic creep response by redistributing stress and activating new energy dissipation pathways.
proposals.method: We will subject coal samples to controlled thermal treatments (25–300°C) to generate crack networks. Time-dependent nanoindentation will quantify changes in creep compliance and hardness at the micro-scale. High-resolution SEM imaging will capture the 2D crack morphology, which will be analyzed using box-counting and multifractal algorithms to compute fractal dimension (D_f) and lacunarity. These microstructural parameters will be correlated with macro-scale three-point bending fracture toughness (K_IC) tests. A statistical model will link D_f to the measured toughness and creep indices.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative framework linking the fractal geometry of thermal cracks to the competing mechanical properties of strength and toughness in coal. It will deliver predictive correlations for engineering design and challenge the singularly negative view of thermal damage by identifying regimes where toughness is enhanced.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying a coupled microscopic-fractal approach to test the non-intuitive hypothesis that thermal 'damage' can concurrently reduce strength but increase toughness, a nuanced mechanical trade-off rarely investigated in heterogeneous carbonaceous materials.

proposals.title: Fractal Characterization of Thermal Void Nucleation and Growth in Quartz and Feldspar Under Cyclic Thermal Loading: Implications for Enhanced Geothermal and Coalbed Methane Recovery
proposals.motivation: In the context of China's carbon neutrality goals, enhancing the efficiency of geothermal energy and coalbed methane recovery is critical. Thermal fracturing, induced by cyclic heating and cooling, is a promising method to create complex fracture networks in tight reservoirs. However, the fundamental, time-dependent (creep) mechanisms of micro-damage initiation—specifically the nucleation and growth of thermal voids in key rock-forming minerals like quartz and feldspar—remain poorly quantified, limiting predictive control of the fracturing process.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that thermal void evolution in quartz and feldspar under cyclic loading follows distinct, fractal scaling laws, and that the cumulative void growth exhibits a power-law relationship with the number of thermal cycles, directly linked to the macroscopic creep and fracturing behavior of the host rock.
proposals.method: We will employ high-temperature nanoindentation coupled with in-situ scanning electron microscopy (SEM) to apply controlled thermal cycles to pure quartz and feldspar samples. The evolution of surface and sub-surface voids will be tracked using high-resolution image analysis. A fractal dimension (D) analysis will be performed on the void patterns extracted from SEM images using the box-counting algorithm. The temporal progression of D and void density will be modeled against cycle count to establish kinetic growth laws, linking microscopic observations to macroscopic creep models.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, mechanistic model for thermal damage accumulation in reservoir rocks at the mineral scale. It will directly inform the design of thermal stimulation protocols for enhanced geothermal systems (EGS) and coalbed methane extraction by predicting optimal cycling parameters to maximize conductive fracture networks while minimizing energy input.
proposals.novelty: While fractal analysis has been applied to hydraulic fractures, its application to quantify the *evolution* of thermally induced micro-voids in specific minerals across cycles is novel. This bridges the gap between microscopic time-dependent mechanical studies (nanoindentation creep) and field-scale multiple injection techniques for fracture network stimulation.

proposals.title: Full-Field Micro-Shear Strain Evolution in Coal and Shale Particles: A Digital Image Correlation and Fractal Analysis of Creep and Fracture
proposals.motivation: Understanding the time-dependent micro-mechanical behavior of geomaterials like coal and shale is critical for predicting long-term stability in carbon storage, unconventional gas extraction, and coal mine disaster reduction. Pre-2026, nanoindentation studies provide point measurements but lack full-field, real-time strain mapping during shear, which is the dominant failure mode in subsurface fractures. Bridging this gap is essential to link microscopic creep and fracturing processes to macroscopic reservoir performance.
proposals.hypothesis: The application of high-resolution Digital Image Correlation (DIC) to direct micro-shear tests will reveal that the evolution of shear strain localization follows a fractal scaling law, and that this strain localization is the primary precursor to time-dependent creep rupture in coal and shale particles.
proposals.method: We will conduct micro-scale shear tests on polished coal and shale specimens within a scanning electron microscope (SEM) or using a high-resolution optical setup. A speckle pattern will be applied to the specimen surface. A commercial DIC software package (e.g., VIC-2D, LaVision DaVis) will be used to compute full-field displacement and strain tensors at sub-micron resolution. The resulting strain localization patterns will be quantitatively analyzed using box-counting and correlation dimension algorithms to calculate their fractal dimension over time.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, full-field maps of shear strain evolution in coal and shale at the micro-scale, directly linking localized deformation to fracture initiation. It will establish a novel micro-mechanical parameter—the fractal dimension of shear strain localization—for predicting the creep and fracturing behavior of heterogeneous geomaterials, advancing models for gas flow and long-term containment.
proposals.novelty: While DIC is common in materials science, its application to map full-field micro-strain during direct shear tests on natural, heterogeneous coal/shale particles is novel. The integration of DIC-derived strain fields with fractal analysis to characterize time-dependent creep damage mechanisms represents a significant methodological advance beyond static nanoindentation or macroscopic testing.

proposals.title: Mapping the microscale thermo-mechanical degradation of coal and shale minerals using post-thermal cycling nanoindentation
proposals.motivation: The transition towards carbon neutrality necessitates a deep understanding of the long-term integrity of geological formations for carbon storage, enhanced gas recovery, and geothermal energy. Coal and shale, as complex heterogeneous geomaterials, undergo significant but poorly quantified microstructural and mechanical property changes when subjected to thermal cycles from injection processes or subsurface heat exchange. Pre-2026, the microscale, time-dependent evolution of mechanical properties (hardness, modulus) post-thermal exposure remains a critical knowledge gap for predicting reservoir stability and fracture network sustainability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that thermal cycling induces spatially heterogeneous, mineral-specific degradation in coal and shale matrices, which can be quantitatively mapped via nanoindentation, and that this degradation follows a fractal scaling law correlating with increased micro-fracturing and altered creep susceptibility.
proposals.method: We will subject polished coal and shale samples to controlled thermal cycling (e.g., 20-200°C, multiple cycles) simulating in-situ thermal perturbations. Post-cycling, we will perform high-spatial-resolution grid nanoindentation (e.g., 100x100 µm grids, Berkovich tip) using a standard Oliver-Pharr analysis to extract hardness (H) and reduced modulus (Er) maps. Statistical deconvolution (Gaussian Mixture Modeling) will identify mechanical phases corresponding to specific minerals (e.g., clay, quartz, organic matter). We will analyze the spatial distribution of degraded zones using fractal dimension (box-counting method) and correlate results with pre- and post-cycling SEM imaging of micro-fracture networks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, mineral-specific maps of thermo-mechanical property evolution in coal/shale, directly linking thermal history to microscale mechanical degradation. It will establish a foundational dataset for modeling long-term geomechanical performance in thermal recovery and carbon storage projects, and offer a novel methodological framework for assessing material durability under cyclic thermal loads.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct, spatially resolved application of nanoindentation *after* thermal cycling to probe induced property changes, combined with a fractal analysis of degradation patterns, moving beyond static property measurement to assess thermal fatigue at the micro-scale in geomaterials.

proposals.title: Linking Acoustic Emission Signatures to Microscopic Creep Fracture in Coal Under In-Situ Shear: A Nano-to-Microscale Investigation
proposals.motivation: Understanding the time-dependent creep and fracture of coal is critical for predicting long-term stability in carbon storage reservoirs and mitigating gas disasters in deep coal mines. Pre-2026, nanoindentation studies have quantified shale's viscoelasticity, but bridging the gap between nanoscale deformation and macroscopic, shear-induced fracture networks in coal remains a challenge. High-frequency acoustic emission (AE) monitoring of micro-specimens under in-situ conditions offers a pathway to directly correlate microscale fracture events with macroscopic failure precursors.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the AE event rate, amplitude, and frequency spectrum during in-situ shear of coal micro-specimens will exhibit a fractal scaling law, directly linked to the progressive nucleation and coalescence of creep-induced micro-fractures observed at the particle scale.
proposals.method: We will conduct in-situ shear tests on polished coal micro-specimens (∼1-3 mm) within a scanning electron microscope (SEM) chamber equipped with a piezoelectric nanoindenter/shear stage. Two high-frequency (1-5 MHz) AE sensors will be coupled to the specimen holder. AE waveforms will be continuously recorded during quasi-static and creep-hold shear segments. The AE data will be analyzed using a combination of Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) for spectral analysis and a modified b-value analysis (fractal dimension of event magnitude-frequency distribution) to characterize fracture scaling. Correlative microscopy will link individual AE events to specific fracture initiation sites at grain boundaries or within organic matter.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a quantitative, physics-based link between acoustic emission signatures and the microscopic mechanics of coal fracture under shear, a key mechanism for gas outbursts. It will contribute a novel micro-scale AE dataset that validates and refines existing fractal fracture models for heterogeneous geomaterials. The findings will directly inform the design of multiple injection techniques for complex fracture networks by identifying the critical micro-fracture density and energy release rates preceding macroscopic failure.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct, simultaneous observation of microscale fracture propagation and high-frequency AE emission in coal under in-situ shear, enabling a causal rather than correlative link between AE metrics and specific fracture mechanisms. Integrating nanoindentation-based mechanical characterization with micro-scale AE monitoring for creep fracture is an unexplored synthesis of Dr. Xie's prior methodologies.

proposals.title: Meso-scale creep and fracture evolution in heterogeneous coal-rock composites under coupled thermo-hydro-mechanical-chemical conditions
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on coal and shale mechanics, including Heping Xie's work, has extensively characterized nano/micro-scale properties via nanoindentation and studied field-scale fracturing. However, a critical knowledge gap exists at the meso-scale (millimeter to centimeter), where the collective behavior of mineral grains, organic matter, and their interfaces dictates the macroscopic time-dependent (creep) deformation and fracture complexity crucial for carbon sequestration integrity, coalbed methane extraction, and gas disaster reduction. Understanding this scale is vital for upscaling microscopic findings and downscaling field observations to predict long-term reservoir behavior under realistic subsurface conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: The meso-scale creep behavior and fracture network complexity in coal-rock composites are governed not by bulk averages but by the statistical distribution and interfacial strength of heterogeneous constituents, leading to a scale-dependent, fractal fracture evolution that deviates from predictions based on homogeneous or purely micro-scale models.
proposals.method: We will fabricate meso-scale composite specimens (∼10-50 mm) replicating the mineralogy and fabric of target coals/shales. Using a custom environmental chamber integrated with a servo-controlled loading frame, we will subject specimens to coupled thermo-hydro-mechanical-chemical (THMC) conditions simulating in-situ stress, pore pressure, temperature, and CO2-rich brine. Time-dependent deformation (creep) will be monitored via digital image correlation (DIC) and acoustic emission (AE) sensors. Post-test, fracture networks will be analyzed using X-ray micro-computed tomography (μCT). Data will be quantified using 3D fractal dimension analysis of fracture surfaces and a statistical volume element (SVE) approach to correlate mechanical response with quantified heterogeneity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first experimentally-grounded framework for upscaling time-dependent mechanical properties from micro to reservoir scale in heterogeneous carbon-bearing strata. It will deliver a predictive model linking meso-scale heterogeneity to creep rates and fracture pattern complexity, directly informing the design of safer CO2 geo-sequestration sites and more effective fracture stimulation strategies for coalbed methane with reduced gas outburst risks.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies focus on either homogeneous macro-specimens or isolated micro-constituents, this proposal uniquely investigates the *interactive* creep and fracturing of a *synthesized heterogeneous composite* at the critically understudied meso-scale under fully coupled THMC conditions, bridging a fundamental scale gap in geomechanics.

proposals.title: Thermal-Pressure Healing of Microcracks in Coal: A Nanoindentation and Fractal Analysis of Strength Recovery Mechanisms
proposals.motivation: Thermal fracturing is a key technique for enhancing permeability in coalbed methane and geothermal reservoirs, but the long-term stability of these engineered fractures is uncertain. Under in-situ stress and geothermal gradients, subsequent thermal cycles could potentially induce crack healing, compromising permeability and affecting reservoir management strategies. Understanding this dual process of thermal damage and healing is critical for predicting the evolution of coal strength and transport properties in energy extraction and carbon storage applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that controlled thermal cycling under confining pressure can induce time-dependent, stress-driven mass transport (e.g., carbonaceous material flow, mineral precipitation) that partially heals thermally-induced microcracks in coal, leading to measurable recovery of mechanical strength at the microscale.
proposals.method: We will subject coal samples to initial thermal shock to generate a baseline microcrack network, characterized using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM). Samples will then undergo multiple thermal cycles (e.g., 50-150°C) under controlled triaxial pressure in a laboratory reactor. The evolution of surface microcracks will be quantified using fractal dimension analysis of SEM images (box-counting algorithm). The key mechanical outcome—strength recovery—will be directly measured via nanoindentation creep tests on healed regions, analyzing changes in hardness, reduced modulus, and creep compliance compared to pre- and post-shock states.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, micromechanical evidence for pressure-assisted thermal healing in coal, linking fractal surface evolution to recovered mechanical properties. It will deliver a conceptual model for the 'healing efficiency' of thermal cycles, informing predictions of long-term fracture network stability in coal reservoirs for gas extraction and CO2 sequestration under carbon neutrality goals.
proposals.novelty: While thermal fracturing is well-studied, the intentional investigation of its reverse process—thermal-pressure healing—as a strength restoration mechanism in coal is novel. The integration of nanoindentation-based creep analysis with fractal quantification of crack morphology evolution offers a new, multi-scale methodology to study this phenomenon.

proposals.title: In-situ Visualization of Phase Behavior in Supercritical/Trilateral Organic Rankine Cycles Using High-Pressure Geophysics Techniques
proposals.motivation: Supercritical and trilateral cycles promise higher efficiency for waste heat recovery from industrial and engine sources, but their design is hindered by a lack of fundamental experimental data on the complex, non-equilibrium phase transitions and heat transfer of working fluids under rapid, high-pressure conditions. Pre-2026 research relies heavily on simulation, with limited validation, creating uncertainty in system optimization and scaling.
proposals.hypothesis: That the application of high-pressure materials science diagnostics, analogous to those used in geophysics, will reveal previously unobserved, time-dependent phase evolution and transport phenomena in organic working fluids under engine waste heat conditions, directly validating or challenging current thermodynamic models.
proposals.method: We will design and construct a modular, optically accessible high-pressure test rig capable of replicating the rapid pressure-temperature transients of an ORC evaporator. Inspired by diamond anvil cell methodologies, we will integrate in-situ laser-induced fluorescence (LIF) and high-speed micro-particle image velocimetry (μ-PIV) to visualize and quantify real-time phase boundaries, nucleation sites, and fluid motion. The experimental data will be analyzed using fractal dimension analysis to characterize the complexity of the phase front evolution and correlated with time-dependent mechanical (creep/relaxation) models adapted from shale nanoindentation studies to understand fluid-structure interaction.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, time-resolved experimental dataset on phase behavior in supercritical/trilateral cycles under dynamic engine conditions. It will yield validated correlations for heat transfer and nucleation, directly informing the design of more efficient heat exchangers and cycle configurations, thereby advancing the practical implementation of high-efficiency waste heat recovery systems.
proposals.novelty: The novel adaptation of high-pressure geophysical diagnostic techniques (e.g., LIF, μ-PIV) from static material study to dynamic fluid systems, combined with fractal analysis of phase fronts, creates a unique experimental paradigm for ORC research, moving beyond post-process analysis to real-time, in-situ observation.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Coupled Thermo-Hydraulic Hysteresis in Coal Gas Desorption: An Integrated Nanoindentation and Fractal Analysis
proposals.motivation: The desorption hysteresis of methane in coal is a critical factor for coalbed methane recovery and carbon geo-sequestration, yet its dependence on in-situ reservoir conditions remains poorly quantified. Understanding how temperature and moisture content, which vary significantly during production and CO2 injection, influence hysteresis is essential for optimizing gas extraction and predicting long-term carbon storage integrity in the context of China's carbon neutrality goals.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that increased temperature reduces the desorption hysteresis index by enhancing molecular mobility, while increased moisture content amplifies hysteresis by blocking pore networks and altering surface energy, with the combined effect being non-linear and fractal-dimension dependent.
proposals.method: We will conduct controlled desorption experiments on coal samples across a temperature range (25-80°C) and moisture saturation levels (0-100%). The desorption hysteresis index will be calculated from high-pressure methane adsorption/desorption isotherms. Concurrently, nanoindentation creep tests will measure time-dependent mechanical properties (hardness, modulus) under the same conditions. Fractal dimensions of the pore structure (Df) will be derived from low-pressure N2 adsorption data using the Frenkel-Halsey-Hill model, correlating Df with hysteresis and mechanical softening.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a quantitative, mechanistic model linking reservoir thermo-hydraulic conditions to gas desorption efficiency and coal mechanical creep. It will provide actionable data for engineering complex fracture networks in coal reservoirs by predicting how hysteresis affects gas release and matrix shrinkage/swelling, directly informing strategies for enhanced gas recovery and disaster reduction.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct, experimental coupling of macroscopic desorption hysteresis with microscopic, time-dependent mechanical properties and fractal pore geometry under varying environmental conditions, an integrated approach not previously applied to this problem.

proposals.title: Quantifying Methane Trapping in Coal Seams via Ink-Bottle Pore Hysteresis: A Coupled Fractal and Nanoindentation Approach
proposals.motivation: Methane trapping in coal micropores is a critical factor controlling both coalbed methane recovery efficiency and greenhouse gas emissions from abandoned mines. Existing models often oversimplify pore structure, failing to capture the hysteresis and entrapment mechanisms responsible for significant residual gas, which impacts resource estimates and carbon neutrality strategies for the coal industry.
proposals.hypothesis: The hysteresis in methane adsorption-desorption isotherms in coal is primarily governed by ink-bottle pore geometry, and the degree of trapping can be quantitatively predicted by adapting cement-based ink-bottle models calibrated with fractal surface descriptors and time-dependent mechanical properties.
proposals.method: We will adapt thermodynamic ink-bottle pore models from cement science, integrating them with fractal dimension analysis of pore surfaces from SEM/BET data. Nanoindentation creep tests on shale and coal samples will provide time-dependent mechanical parameters (e.g., hardness, modulus, creep compliance) to inform the stress-dependent evolution of pore accessibility. A numerical algorithm will be developed to simulate adsorption-desorption cycles, using the fractal dimension to scale the ink-bottle neck/body ratio distribution and the mechanical parameters to model aperture changes under effective stress.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, physically grounded model for predicting unrecoverable methane in coal, directly contributing to improved gas-in-place estimates and enhanced coalbed methane production strategies. It will also offer a new mechanistic understanding of methane retention relevant for assessing greenhouse gas emissions from coal mines under carbon neutrality goals.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the cross-disciplinary application of a mature cement science pore model to coal methane trapping, uniquely coupled with fractal surface characterization and time-dependent nanoindentation data to dynamically constrain pore accessibility.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Relative Contributions of Chemical Alteration and Thermal Cracking to the Weakening of Coal and Shale in Enhanced Geothermal Systems
proposals.motivation: The development of Enhanced Geothermal Systems (EGS) and the geological storage of CO2 in coal seams rely on understanding the long-term evolution of reservoir rock strength under thermal and chemical loads. Pre-2026 research often attributes permeability enhancement and strength reduction primarily to thermal stress-induced fracturing from cyclic injection. However, the sustained presence of reactive geothermal fluids (e.g., CO2-rich brine) suggests chemical alteration may be a dominant, yet poorly quantified, weakening mechanism, with critical implications for reservoir stability and carbon neutrality goals in fossil fuel transition zones.
proposals.hypothesis: For coal and organic-rich shale under simulated EGS conditions, chemical alteration of mineral and organic matter by reactive geothermal fluids contributes more to the time-dependent reduction in mechanical strength and the evolution of fracture network complexity than do thermal cycles alone.
proposals.method: We will conduct coupled laboratory experiments and microscopic analysis. Core samples of coal and shale will undergo (1) thermal cycling only, (2) static fluid exposure only (CO2-saturated brine at 80-120°C), and (3) combined thermal cycling with fluid exposure. Time-dependent mechanical properties will be characterized via nanoindentation creep tests and triaxial compression. Post-experiment, we will employ scanning electron microscopy (SEM) with energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) to map chemical alteration (dissolution, precipitation) and micro-crack networks. Fractal dimension analysis of fracture patterns from SEM images will quantify network complexity, allowing statistical correlation between chemical alteration extent, fractal dimension, and measured strength reduction.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative framework to decouple chemical and thermal weakening mechanisms in geo-energy reservoirs. It will deliver predictive correlations between fluid chemistry, alteration mineralogy, and the time-dependent mechanical response of coal and shale. The findings will directly inform more accurate models for EGS design and CO2-enhanced coalbed methane recovery, supporting safer and more efficient subsurface engineering.
proposals.novelty: While thermal fracturing is well-studied, this proposal directly challenges its primacy by quantitatively comparing its effect against in-situ chemical alteration, a process often acknowledged but rarely isolated and measured. The integration of nanoindentation-based creep analysis with fractal quantification of chemically altered surfaces represents a novel, multi-scale approach to this problem.

proposals.title: Grain Boundary-Controlled Shear Failure in Polymineralic Coal and Shale Aggregates: A Coupled Nanoindentation and Fractal Fracture Mechanics Study
proposals.motivation: Conventional models of shear failure in geomaterials often treat mineral grains as isolated mechanical units, neglecting the critical role of grain boundary interactions. In heterogeneous, carbon-rich materials like coal and shale, which are central to energy extraction and carbon storage, these interactions govern time-dependent deformation (creep) and fracture network complexity. A pre-2026 understanding of how polymineralic interfaces control macroscopic shear behavior is essential for predicting reservoir stability and hydraulic fracturing efficiency.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the macroscopic shear strength and creep behavior of polymineralic coal/shale aggregates are governed not by the strength of individual minerals, but by the fractal geometry and time-dependent frictional properties of their grain boundary networks.
proposals.method: We will employ high-resolution nanoindentation with a Berkovich tip to perform grid indentation and in-situ creep tests on polished coal and shale samples, mapping the mechanical properties (hardness, reduced modulus, creep compliance) of individual minerals and, crucially, across grain boundaries. The resulting fracture patterns from micro-scale scratch tests along boundaries will be analyzed using box-counting and multifractal analysis to quantify their fractal dimension. A discrete element model (DEM) will be constructed, informed by the nanoindentation data, to simulate shear failure where particle interactions are governed by a time-dependent, fractal-based cohesive zone law for boundaries.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic, grain-boundary-focused framework for predicting shear failure in heterogeneous carbonaceous rocks, directly linking microscopic interface properties to macroscopic fracture network development. It will deliver a novel DEM modeling approach that incorporates experimentally derived fractal and time-dependent boundary laws, advancing beyond homogeneous or isolated mineral assumptions in geomechanical prediction.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly shifting the shear failure locus from mineral grains to their boundaries, and in quantitatively characterizing these boundaries using coupled nanoindentation creep tests and fractal fracture analysis—a synthesis not previously applied to coal/shale polymineralic systems.

proposals.title: The Role of Biotite Plastic Slip in Enhancing Ductility and Fracture Network Connectivity in Coal-Bearing Shales: A Coupled Nano- to Macro-Scale Investigation
proposals.motivation: Understanding the mechanical behavior of coal-bearing shales is critical for predicting subsurface deformation, hydraulic fracturing efficiency, and long-term reservoir stability in energy extraction and carbon storage. Pre-2026 research on creep and fracturing often treats clay-rich phases as weaknesses, but the specific, potentially beneficial role of biotite's time-dependent plastic slip in modulating rock ductility and fracture propagation is poorly quantified. This gap limits our ability to engineer fracture networks for enhanced gas recovery or CO2 sequestration in complex lithologies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that under sustained deviatoric stress, the development of plastic slip bands within biotite grains acts as a distributed energy dissipation mechanism, enhancing the macroscopic ductility of the rock and promoting the connectivity of brittle fracture networks by deflecting and bridging microcracks.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-scale experimental and analytical approach. First, targeted nanoindentation creep tests on individual biotite grains and biotite-shale interfaces will quantify time-dependent plastic slip parameters. Second, in-situ uniaxial and triaxial creep tests on shale samples with varying biotite content will be conducted, with concurrent Acoustic Emission (AE) monitoring to track microfracture evolution. Post-experiment, we will use scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and fractal analysis (box-counting method) to characterize the morphology and connectivity of fracture networks and correlate them with the observed plastic slip bands. A micromechanical model, incorporating the nanoindentation-derived biotite rheology into a representative volume element, will be developed to upscale the findings.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic, quantitative link between biotite's microscale plastic deformation and the macroscale creep and fracturing behavior of shales. It will deliver predictive criteria for how biotite content and orientation influence fracture network complexity, directly informing models for hydraulic fracturing design and long-term geomechanical risk assessment in carbon-neutral energy projects.
proposals.novelty: This proposal inverts the conventional view of biotite as merely a brittle flaw by investigating its plastic slip as a ductility-enhancing and fracture-connecting mechanism, a perspective not systematically explored in pre-2026 shale mechanics literature. It uniquely couples nanoindentation-derived biotite rheology with macroscopic fractal analysis of fracture networks.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Permeability Enhancement from Catastrophic Brittle Failure in Quartz and Feldspar: A Microscopic and Fractal Analysis
proposals.motivation: In coalbed methane and shale gas extraction, hydraulic fracturing aims to create permeable fracture networks, but catastrophic failure events are typically viewed as undesirable due to seismic risk and loss of control. However, pre-2026 studies on rock mechanics suggest that brittle, catastrophic failure in minerals like quartz and feldspar may generate exceptionally complex, high-surface-area fracture patterns. This research challenges the prevailing negative paradigm by investigating whether such failure modes could be strategically leveraged to enhance reservoir permeability more effectively than controlled, stable fracturing.
proposals.hypothesis: Catastrophic brittle failure in quartz and feldspar, the dominant brittle minerals in many reservoirs, creates fracture networks with significantly higher fractal dimensions and surface area per unit volume compared to stable, sub-critical crack growth, leading to a net positive impact on long-term permeability for fluid flow.
proposals.method: We will conduct controlled uniaxial compression tests on synthetic quartz-feldspar composite samples and natural sandstone cores, instrumented with acoustic emission to capture failure dynamics. Post-failure, we will employ high-resolution scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and micro-CT scanning to obtain 3D images of the fracture networks. Fractal dimension (Df) of the fracture surfaces and the fracture network will be calculated using the box-counting algorithm on binarized 2D SEM images and 3D micro-CT data. Permeability changes will be quantified via core-flooding experiments on failed samples, correlating flow rates with the measured fractal parameters and surface area derived from image analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, mechanistic link between catastrophic failure modes, fractal fracture geometry, and permeability evolution, offering a new perspective on fracture design. It will deliver a novel framework for assessing the trade-offs between seismic risk and reservoir stimulation efficiency, directly informing strategies for gas extraction and carbon sequestration in brittle geological formations.
proposals.novelty: The proposal fundamentally re-frames catastrophic failure from a purely hazardous phenomenon to a potential engineering mechanism for permeability creation, a concept not explored in pre-2026 literature. It uniquely integrates real-time mechanical testing, high-resolution microscopic imaging, and fractal analysis specifically to test this counter-intuitive hypothesis.

proposals.title: A Multi-Scale Fractal and Creep Constitutive Model for Coal Reservoir Shear Failure: From Nanoindentation to Reservoir-Scale Fracture Network Prediction
proposals.motivation: Predicting the long-term shear behavior and fracture network complexity of coal reservoirs is critical for safe and efficient coalbed methane extraction and carbon geo-sequestration, especially under China's carbon neutrality goals. However, a persistent gap exists between micro-scale measurements of time-dependent mechanical properties (e.g., via nanoindentation) and macro-scale reservoir geomechanical models, limiting our ability to forecast reservoir-scale shear failure and its impact on permeability evolution.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the macroscopic shear failure and associated fracture network development in heterogeneous coal reservoirs can be accurately predicted by upscaling micro-scale mineral-interface failure laws, where the creep and fracture properties of constituent phases and their interfaces govern the emergent, time-dependent reservoir-scale shear behavior.
proposals.method: The research will integrate: 1) Nanoindentation creep tests on coal macerals and mineral inclusions to derive phase-specific viscoelastic-plastic parameters. 2) Development of a 3D micro-mechanical Representative Volume Element (RVE) model using the Finite Element Method (FEM), incorporating cohesive zone models for interfaces and fractal geometry to characterize initial micro-crack distributions. 3) Homogenization of the RVE response to formulate a macroscopic, time-dependent constitutive law. 4) Implementation of this law into a discrete fracture network (DFN) simulator to predict reservoir-scale shear failure and permeability changes under in-situ stress conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, physics-based multi-scale modeling framework that directly links measurable micro-scale properties to reservoir geomechanical forecasts. It will provide a quantitative tool for optimizing hydraulic fracturing designs in coal reservoirs for gas extraction and for assessing the long-term shear integrity of coal seams targeted for CO2 storage.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit integration of time-dependent (creep) micro-mechanical data, fractal characterization of micro-fractures, and interface failure mechanics into a cohesive upscaling procedure specifically for heterogeneous coal, moving beyond empirical correlations or purely elastic upscaling methods common in pre-2026 studies.

proposals.title: Quantifying Energy Dissipation in Biotite Interlayer Plastic Slip: A Coupled Nanoindentation and Fractal Analysis Approach for Subsurface Deformation Prediction
proposals.motivation: Biotite, a common phyllosilicate in coal-bearing strata and geothermal reservoirs, significantly influences the time-dependent creep and fracture behavior of rocks through its weak interlayer slip. Pre-2026, the precise energy dissipation mechanisms of this slip under coupled thermo-mechanical loading, critical for predicting long-term reservoir stability and fracture network evolution, remain poorly quantified. This gap limits our ability to model energy budgets in subsurface engineering for carbon storage and geothermal energy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the energy dissipated by biotite interlayer slip under shear and thermal cycling follows a power-law scaling with slip displacement, characterized by a fractal dimension that evolves with temperature and loading rate, governing the transition from distributed creep to localized fracturing.
proposals.method: We will employ high-temperature nanoindentation with a Berkovich tip and a custom micro-shear stage to apply controlled cyclic shear loads on isolated biotite grains under temperatures up to 300°C. The dissipated energy per cycle will be calculated from the hysteresis area of load-displacement curves. We will analyze the spatial and temporal patterns of slip using Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) and Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM), quantifying surface roughness evolution via the box-counting fractal dimension (D). A correlation model between D, dissipated energy, and thermal cycles will be developed.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, micro-mechanical energy dissipation law for biotite slip, directly informing constitutive models for shale and coal creep. It will establish a novel micro-to-macro link by correlating fractal surface degradation with energy loss, enhancing predictions of fracture network complexity and long-term seal integrity in carbon-neutral subsurface projects.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct, experimental quantification of energy dissipation for a specific mineral slip system under coupled thermal-mechanical cycling, and the integration of nanoindentation-derived energy metrics with fractal analysis of resulting surface damage—a synthesis not previously applied to phyllosilicate plasticity.

proposals.title: Quantitative Mapping of Mineral-Matrix Interface Strength in Coal and Shale via Nanoindentation: A Precursor to Shear Failure Analysis
proposals.motivation: The long-term creep and fracturing behavior of coal and shale, critical for carbon sequestration, gas extraction, and mine stability, is governed by the mechanical properties of heterogeneous microstructures, particularly the interfaces between minerals and the organic matrix. Pre-2026, while nanoindentation is established for bulk phase properties, a systematic methodology to quantitatively map and predict the strength of these critical interfaces prior to macroscopic shear failure is lacking. This gap limits our ability to model and engineer subsurface systems for energy and environmental goals under China's carbon neutrality transition.
proposals.hypothesis: The spatial distribution of interface strength, measured via targeted nanoindentation at phase boundaries, is a primary control on the initiation and propagation pathways of shear fractures in heterogeneous geomaterials. We hypothesize that a pre-shear 'strength map' can predict the location and mode of interfacial debonding.
proposals.method: We will employ high-spatial-resolution grid nanoindentation on polished coal/shale samples to create mechanical property maps (hardness H, reduced modulus Er). Using pre-2026 established protocols (e.g., Oliver-Pharr method, statistical deconvolution), we will first identify mechanical phases. Critically, we will perform targeted indentation arrays directly across visible mineral-matrix interfaces. The interface strength will be quantified by analyzing pop-in events in load-displacement curves and calculating the interfacial shear strength using a pre-2026 analytical model that relates indentation stress fields to interfacial debonding. This data will be correlated with post-mortem microscopic and fractal analysis of shear-induced fracture networks from subsequent direct shear tests on the same samples.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, quantitative protocol for pre-failure interface strength characterization in geomaterials. It will directly link microscale interfacial mechanics to macroscale shear fracturing patterns, providing essential input parameters for more predictive constitutive models of creep and fracture in coal and shale. The findings will inform strategies for engineered fracturing in coalbed methane recovery and the assessment of caprock integrity for carbon storage.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the proactive use of nanoindentation not just for phase identification, but as a direct quantitative mapping tool for *interface* strength prior to shear testing, creating a predictive 'mechanical map' of failure precursors. This integrates Heping Xie's expertise in nanoindentation, fracturing, and fractal analysis into a new, targeted methodology for interfacial mechanics.

proposals.title: A Peridynamic-Fractal Model for Simulating Thermally-Induced Micro-Cracking and Creep in Coal Minerals
proposals.motivation: The transition to carbon neutrality pressures the coal industry to develop technologies like underground coal gasification (UCG) and enhanced geothermal systems (EGS) in coal seams, where thermal cycling induces complex micro-cracking that controls gas flow and long-term creep stability. Existing continuum fracture mechanics struggles to model spontaneous, complex crack nucleation and branching in heterogeneous mineral aggregates under thermal fatigue, a critical gap for predicting reservoir stimulation and disaster reduction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a state-based peridynamic model, calibrated with nanoindentation creep data and incorporating the fractal geometry of pre-existing micro-fractures, can accurately simulate the initiation, propagation, and network formation of thermal fatigue cracks in coal minerals, revealing their controlling influence on time-dependent mechanical behavior.
proposals.method: We will develop a 2D/3D state-based peridynamic model using an open-source framework (e.g., Peridigm). The model will represent coal as a heterogeneous medium of organic matter and mineral particles. Material properties (bond stiffness, critical stretch) will be derived from pre-2026 nanoindentation creep tests on shale and coal. Thermal fatigue will be simulated via cyclic temperature boundary conditions, inducing bond breakage. We will incorporate initial crack networks with fractal dimensions quantified from SEM images of coal samples. The simulation will track crack path complexity, growth rate, and their correlation with applied thermal stress and mineral geometry.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel computational tool to predict micro-crack evolution under thermal loads in coal, directly linking microscopic fracture processes to macroscopic creep and permeability change. It will offer mechanistic insights for optimizing thermal stimulation techniques (e.g., in UCG) to generate complex fracture networks while managing long-term geomechanical risks, contributing to both gas extraction and disaster reduction.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of peridynamics—a non-local fracture mechanics method adept at modeling discontinuous crack paths—with the fractal characterization of real coal microstructures and time-dependent properties from nanoindentation, specifically to solve the problem of thermal fatigue cracking in energy-related coal applications.

proposals.title: Atomic-Scale Shear Failure Mechanisms in Biotite Layers: A Molecular Dynamics Study of Creep and Fracture Initiation
proposals.motivation: The time-dependent deformation (creep) and fracturing of coal and shale, critical for gas extraction and geomechanical stability, are governed by microscale processes in clay minerals like biotite. Pre-2026 nanoindentation studies on shale highlight time-dependent mechanical behavior, but the atomic-scale initiation of shear failure at biotite layer contacts remains poorly understood. This gap limits our ability to predict long-term reservoir behavior and design effective fracturing strategies for coalbed methane and enhanced gas recovery.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the initiation of shear failure at atomic contacts between biotite layers is governed by a critical interplay of interfacial water structure, cation distribution (e.g., K+), and applied shear strain rate, which dictates the transition from stable creep to localized fracture nucleation.
proposals.method: We will employ classical molecular dynamics (MD) simulations using the LAMMPS package with a CLAYFF force field, validated for phyllosilicates. Atomic models of biotite stacks will be constructed, and shear deformation will be applied under controlled normal loads. Simulations will systematically vary environmental conditions (water saturation, interlayer cation type) and loading rates. The analysis will quantify shear stress evolution, atomic displacement fields, and the evolution of coordination numbers to identify failure nucleation criteria.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first atomic-scale mechanistic model for shear failure initiation in biotite, directly linking to macroscopic creep and fracture phenomena in coal-bearing strata. It will deliver quantitative parameters, such as critical shear stresses and activation energies for slip, that can inform upscaled constitutive models for reservoir simulation and fracturing design, bridging the gap between nanoscale mechanics and field-scale engineering.
proposals.novelty: While MD has been used to study clay minerals, its targeted application to simulate the *initiation* of shear failure in biotite under conditions relevant to coal reservoir geomechanics, integrating insights from recent nanoindentation and fracturing studies, is novel. This approach moves beyond equilibrium property calculation to explicitly model the dynamic, rate-dependent failure process.

proposals.title: A Multi-Scale Discrete Fracture Network Model for Coal Reservoir Permeability Evolution: Integrating Nanoindentation-Derived Creep Laws and Fractal Particle Mechanics
proposals.motivation: Accurately predicting the long-term permeability evolution of coal reservoirs is critical for both enhanced coalbed methane recovery and carbon geo-sequestration, yet current discrete fracture network (DFN) models often rely on empirical or simplified macro-scale failure criteria. This gap is significant because the time-dependent, micro-scale mechanical behavior of coal and shale constituents, including creep and sub-critical crack growth, fundamentally controls meso-scale fracture network development and connectivity under sustained in-situ stresses. A physics-based upscaling approach is needed to bridge micro-scale mechanisms and reservoir-scale performance.
proposals.hypothesis: Incorporating micro-scale, time-dependent failure rules derived from nanoindentation creep tests and fractal descriptions of particle-scale damage into a meso-scale DFN framework will significantly improve the prediction of coal reservoir permeability evolution under true triaxial stress and fluid injection conditions.
proposals.method: We will first conduct nanoindentation creep tests on coal samples to extract time-hardening or power-law creep parameters at the micro-scale. These parameters will inform a probabilistic, time-to-failure rule for bond breakage within a lattice-based or synthetic DFN model at the meso-scale (cm to m). The generation of initial fracture networks will be guided by fractal dimension analysis of particle distributions from microscopic studies. The model will be implemented using a coupled hydro-mechanical code (e.g., based on UDEC or a custom Python solver) to simulate fracture propagation and network connectivity evolution under multiple fluid injection scenarios, with permeability calculated from the evolving fracture aperture field.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, multi-scale DFN model that explicitly links measurable micro-scale rock properties to reservoir-scale permeability forecasts. It will provide a quantitative tool for optimizing injection strategies in coal reservoirs for gas extraction or CO2 storage, directly addressing the technological needs highlighted by China's carbon neutrality goals. The methodology will also advance fundamental understanding of how creep and heterogeneous microstructures govern fracture network complexity.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct integration of nanoindentation-derived, time-dependent constitutive laws into a stochastic DFN model, moving beyond static strength criteria. Furthermore, using fractal particle mechanics to condition the initial DFN geometry creates a more physically realistic baseline for simulating injection-induced fracture network stimulation.

proposals.title: Thermo-Chemical Healing of Shear-Induced Micro-Cracks in Coal: Mechanisms and Kinetics from Nano- to Micro-Scale
proposals.motivation: While extensive pre-2026 research focuses on fracture initiation and propagation in coal and shale for resource extraction, the reverse process—fracture healing—is critically underexplored. This gap is significant for carbon neutrality goals, as healed fractures can reduce permeability, impacting CO2 sequestration integrity in coal seams and long-term wellbore stability in abandoned mines. Understanding healing mechanisms is essential for predicting and engineering subsurface system evolution over decadal timescales.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that shear-induced micro-cracks in coal can be healed under specific thermal and chemical conditions via mechanisms including pressure solution, carbonaceous particle re-deposition, and mineral precipitation, and that the healing kinetics and resultant mechanical recovery can be quantified and modeled using fractal and time-dependent constitutive laws.
proposals.method: We will employ nanoindentation creep tests on pre-sheared coal samples to establish baseline time-dependent mechanical properties. Controlled healing experiments will subject these samples to elevated temperatures (50-150°C) and CO2-saturated brine environments in a reaction cell. The evolution of crack morphology will be tracked using high-resolution SEM and atomic force microscopy (AFM) pre- and post-treatment. Fractal dimension analysis of crack networks will quantify healing progress, and a modified Drucker-Prager cap model with a time-dependent healing term will be developed to describe the mechanical recovery.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative framework linking thermal-chemical conditions to the healing kinetics of shear fractures in coal, directly informing models for long-term caprock integrity in CO2 storage. It will advance the fundamental understanding of the dual fracture processes in geo-materials, contributing to safer subsurface engineering and environmental management in the energy transition.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying a microscopic, time-dependent mechanical analysis framework—typically used for studying creep and fracture—to the inverse problem of healing, specifically for shear-induced damage in carbonaceous materials under conditions relevant to carbon management.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Fractal Evolution of Pore-Fracture Networks in Geothermal and Aquifer Systems via Pressure-Preserved Coring and Nanoindentation Creep Analysis
proposals.motivation: The sustainable development of geothermal energy and groundwater resources critically depends on understanding the in-situ mechanical and transport properties of deep, low-permeability reservoirs. Pre-2026, pressure-preserved coring is a nascent technology primarily applied to unconventional oil and gas to capture original reservoir conditions, but its potential for characterizing creep behavior and fracture networks in geothermal and aquifer systems remains unexplored. This gap limits our ability to predict long-term reservoir performance and fluid-rock interactions under carbon-neutral energy transition scenarios.
proposals.hypothesis: Pressure-preserved coring, combined with microscopic mechanical testing, will reveal that the creep behavior and fractal characteristics of pore-fracture networks in geothermal and aquifer rocks are fundamentally distinct from those in hydrocarbon shales, governed by different fluid-rock-chemical interactions and in-situ stress states.
proposals.method: We will employ advanced pressure-preserved coring to retrieve intact samples from target geothermal and deep aquifer formations. Core analysis will integrate micro-CT scanning for 3D pore-fracture network reconstruction and fractal dimension calculation. Time-dependent nanoindentation creep tests, following Xie's established protocols, will be conducted on mineral phases and natural fractures to derive microscopic creep parameters. A coupled analysis will correlate fractal dimensions of the transport network with macroscopic creep compliance and hydraulic properties measured under simulated in-situ conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel methodological framework for assessing the long-term geomechanical stability and flow capacity of non-hydrocarbon subsurface systems. It will provide the first dataset linking in-situ preserved microstructure to time-dependent mechanical properties in geothermal and aquifer rocks, offering critical parameters for predictive models of reservoir stimulation and sustainable management.
proposals.novelty: The novel application of pressure-preserved coring and nanoindentation creep analysis to geothermal and aquifer systems, explicitly linking fractal fracture networks to time-dependent deformation, moves beyond the established oil/gas focus. This integrates Heping Xie's expertise in creep, fracturing, and microscopic methods into the domain of renewable energy and water security.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Time-Dependent Evolution of Fractal Pore Networks in Coal Under Stress Using Integrated High-Resolution Coring and 3D Imaging
proposals.motivation: Accurate pore structure characterization in coal is critical for predicting gas flow, carbon sequestration potential, and long-term mechanical stability, especially under the dual pressures of energy transition and carbon neutrality goals. Existing methods often fail to capture the dynamic, time-dependent (creep) evolution of complex, fractal pore-fracture networks under in-situ stress conditions, limiting predictive models for gas disaster reduction and CO2 storage.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the creep deformation of coal under confining stress systematically alters the fractal dimension and connectivity of its pore network, and that this evolution can be quantitatively characterized by integrating stress-preserved coring with advanced 3D imaging and fractal analysis.
proposals.method: We will employ a novel pressure-coring tool to obtain coal samples preserving in-situ gas and stress. Core analysis will combine pre-2026 advanced techniques: micro-CT and FIB-SEM for 3D pore structure imaging at multiple scales, and nanoindentation creep tests on polished sections to obtain time-dependent mechanical properties. Fractal analysis of the 3D pore networks will be performed using the box-counting algorithm (D_b) and pore size distribution (D_psd) methods. Correlations will be established between fractal dimensions, nanoindentation creep parameters, and stress conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, quantitative link between the microscopic time-dependent mechanical behavior (creep) of coal and the evolution of its transport-effective pore structure. It will provide a validated, multi-scale methodology for predicting long-term fracture network complexity and permeability changes in coal reservoirs for enhanced gas recovery and CO2 geo-sequestration.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct integration of stress-preserved coring with multi-scale 3D fractal analysis specifically to investigate time-dependent pore structure evolution, moving beyond static characterization to a dynamic framework aligned with Heping Xie's focus on creep, fracturing, and carbon in coal.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Time-Dependent Evolution of Fractal Pore-Fracture Networks in Coal Under Stress Using High-Resolution Medical CT Imaging
proposals.motivation: Understanding the coupled creep deformation and gas transport in coal is critical for both coalbed methane (CBM) recovery and carbon geo-sequestration in coal seams, key to China's carbon neutrality transition. The long-term mechanical stability and permeability evolution are governed by the time-dependent restructuring of complex pore-fracture networks, which pre-2026 techniques like nanoindentation or conventional core analysis cannot dynamically visualize in 3D under stress.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the creep behavior of coal is intrinsically linked to a time-dependent, fractal reorganization of its internal pore-fracture network, which can be quantitatively characterized in 3D using in-situ medical CT scanning, revealing non-linear permeability decay mechanisms.
proposals.method: We will employ a high-resolution medical CT scanner (e.g., GE Revolution CT) to perform in-situ, time-lapse micro-CT imaging on preserved coal cores subjected to constant triaxial stress, simulating reservoir conditions. A custom-built, CT-transparent creep cell will enable sequential 4D imaging. The 3D image stacks will be segmented using a hybrid watershed and machine learning algorithm (e.g., Trainable Weka Segmentation in Fiji) to isolate pores and fractures. The evolving network will be analyzed for fractal dimension (using box-counting), connectivity, and aperture distribution over time using open-source pore network analysis software (e.g., PoreSpy and OpenPNM).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first 4D, in-situ visualization and quantitative fractal analysis of creep-induced pore network evolution in coal, directly linking microscopic mechanical changes to macroscopic time-dependent behavior. It will deliver a predictive model for long-term permeability evolution, directly informing strategies for enhanced CBM recovery and the assessment of CO2 storage integrity in coal seams.
proposals.novelty: The novel integration of high-temporal-resolution medical CT imaging with in-situ mechanical loading for coal creep studies is unprecedented, moving beyond static snapshots to dynamic, 4D fractal analysis of a reacting pore network under stress, bridging the gap between Xie's prior nanoindentation work and field-scale fracturing studies.

proposals.title: A Data Fusion Framework for Predicting Time-Dependent Fracture and Flow in Coal and Shale from Cross-Domain Porosity-Strength Correlations
proposals.motivation: Predicting the long-term creep and fracturing behavior of coal and shale under carbon storage or gas extraction conditions is critical for safety and efficiency, but is hindered by the complex, multi-scale nature of natural rock porosity. Pre-2026 materials science databases contain extensive, well-characterized data on the mechanical properties of synthetic porous materials (e.g., metal-organic frameworks, engineered ceramics) as a function of their pore structure. Fusing these disparate datasets can reveal universal porosity-property relationships that are difficult to extract from geotechnical data alone, offering a novel pathway to model subsurface materials.
proposals.hypothesis: That a machine learning model trained on a fused dataset of synthetic material and natural rock porosity descriptors can accurately predict the time-dependent mechanical (creep) and fracture evolution in coal and shale, transcending the limitations of domain-specific models.
proposals.method: We will curate porosity and mechanical property data from materials databases (e.g., the Materials Project, NIST) and from Heping Xie's prior nanoindentation and fracturing studies on coal/shale. Key descriptors will include fractal dimension of pore surfaces, pore size distribution, and specific surface area. A graph neural network (GNN) architecture will be employed to model the irregular, relational structure of the pore network data. The model will be trained to predict creep compliance and fracture network complexity under stress, validated against laboratory-scale hydraulic fracturing experiments on coal samples.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new, transferable informatics framework for predicting geomechanical behavior from microstructural data. It will provide specific, validated correlations between quantifiable porosity metrics and time-dependent fracture propagation in energy-related geomaterials, directly aiding the design of gas extraction and carbon sequestration strategies under China's carbon neutrality goals.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit cross-domain data fusion between well-characterized synthetic porous materials and complex natural rocks to inform geomechanical models, an approach not yet common in pre-2026 geotechnical research, which typically operates within a single material domain.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of a Miniature Pressure-Preserving Sampler for Downscaled Core Flooding Experiments on Fractured Coal and Shale
proposals.motivation: Accurate laboratory-scale core flooding experiments are critical for validating reservoir models used in enhanced coalbed methane recovery and CO2 geo-sequestration. However, conventional coring and sample preparation methods release in-situ pore pressure and stress, altering the micro-fracture networks and surface properties that govern gas flow and creep behavior. A method to obtain small, representative samples while preserving their in-situ pressure state is a significant pre-2026 gap, limiting the fidelity of lab-to-field upscaling.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a miniature pressure-preserving sampler can successfully capture and maintain the in-situ pore pressure and micro-fracture architecture of coal and shale, enabling laboratory core flooding experiments that more accurately replicate reservoir-scale multiphase flow and time-dependent deformation (creep) phenomena.
proposals.method: The research will design and fabricate a miniature sampler using a pressure-compensated, sealed chamber. We will employ micro-CT scanning and nanoindentation techniques (aligned with Xie's recent methods) to characterize the fractal dimensions and micro-fracture networks of samples obtained with the new sampler versus conventional methods. Downscaled core flooding experiments with CO2/water will be conducted under controlled stress, with flow dynamics analyzed using fractal theory and discrete particle/fracture network modeling to quantify differences in transport properties.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel protocol for obtaining representative lab-scale samples under in-situ conditions, directly improving the validation of reservoir simulation models. It will generate new insights into the pressure-dependent evolution of fracture networks and surface interactions during gas flow, contributing to more effective strategies for gas disaster reduction and carbon-neutral utilization of coal reservoirs.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of a miniature pressure-preserving sampling device with fractal analysis of micro-fractures and time-dependent nanoindentation studies, creating a direct, high-fidelity downscaling link between reservoir conditions and laboratory experiments on coal and shale, which has not been systematically achieved pre-2026.

proposals.title: A Multi-Scale Fractal Upscaling Framework for Predicting Gas Flow and Storage in Coal Reservoirs from Nanoindentation Creep Data
proposals.motivation: Accurate prediction of reservoir-scale fluid flow and storage in coalbed methane and CO2 geo-sequestration projects is hindered by the scale gap between laboratory measurements on preserved cores and field-scale behavior. Pre-2026, the industry lacks robust, physics-based methods to upscale time-dependent (creep) and fracture-dependent mechanical properties, which critically control long-term permeability evolution and carbon storage integrity in heterogeneous coal seams.
proposals.hypothesis: The fractal characteristics of coal's pore-fracture networks, quantified at the micro-scale via nanoindentation creep tests, can be used to construct a scale-invariant model that accurately predicts reservoir-scale hydraulic properties and their time-dependent evolution.
proposals.method: We will integrate Heping Xie's established methods: 1) Perform nanoindentation creep tests on preserved coal cores to obtain time-dependent mechanical parameters (hardness, modulus, creep exponent) at the micro-scale. 2) Use high-resolution imaging (SEM, micro-CT) to characterize the fractal dimension (D_f) of pore spaces and fracture networks within the same samples. 3) Develop an upscaling algorithm based on fractal theory and homogenization techniques, explicitly linking the nanoindentation-derived visco-elastic parameters and D_f to a dual-porosity permeability model. 4) Validate the upscaled predictions against historical gas production or injection data from the case study reservoir, calibrating the model using a history-matching approach.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, mechanistic bridge between micro-scale geomechanical measurements and reservoir simulation, directly addressing the carbon industry's need for predictive models of subsurface behavior. It will deliver a practical upscaling workflow that incorporates time-dependent fracturing and creep, improving forecasts for methane recovery and the long-term security of CO2 storage in coal formations.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct coupling of nanoindentation-derived visco-elastic parameters with fractal fracture network data for hydraulic upscaling, a synthesis not previously attempted in coal reservoir characterization, moving beyond static elastic properties to capture essential time-dependent behavior.

proposals.title: Fractal Universality Under Stress: A Nano- to Macro-Scale Investigation of Fracture Pattern Divergence in Anisotropic Coal and Shale
proposals.motivation: The universality of fracture patterns, often described by power-law scaling and fractal dimensions, is a foundational concept in rock mechanics and geophysics, applied to phenomena from hydraulic fracturing to earthquake seismicity. However, its validity in highly heterogeneous and anisotropic crustal rocks like coal and shale, which are critical for energy extraction and carbon storage, remains inadequately tested. Pre-2026, the drive for carbon neutrality intensifies the need to precisely predict fracture behavior in these complex media for safe and efficient subsurface engineering.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the universal structural patterns of fracture, characterized by fractal dimension and scaling exponents, systematically deviate in heterogeneous, anisotropic coal and shale compared to isotropic, homogeneous rock models, due to the influence of inherent microstructural fabrics and compositional layering on time-dependent crack propagation.
proposals.method: We will employ multi-scale mechanical characterization, integrating nanoindentation creep tests on coal and shale samples to obtain time-dependent mechanical properties of constituent particles and laminations. Concurrently, we will conduct controlled laboratory-scale fracturing experiments (e.g., three-point bending, uniaxial compression) on the same materials. Fracture networks from both scales will be analyzed using high-resolution microscopy (SEM) and image processing to extract fractal dimensions (D) using the box-counting algorithm. Statistical analysis will correlate the divergence of D from theoretical universality classes (e.g., D~1.7 for 2D brittle fracture) with quantified anisotropy indices and nanoindentation-derived property maps.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative evidence linking microstructural heterogeneity and anisotropy to macroscopic deviations in fracture pattern universality in energy-relevant rocks. It will deliver a refined, material-specific scaling framework to improve the predictive modeling of complex fracture networks for enhanced coalbed methane recovery and shale gas stimulation, directly supporting the technological transition of the coal industry under carbon constraints.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in directly challenging a bedrock assumption of rock fracture mechanics by coupling time-dependent nano-mechanics with fractal analysis of induced fractures, specifically targeting the anisotropic media (coal/shale) where the assumption's failure would have the greatest practical consequence, a synthesis not yet pursued in pre-2026 literature.
