proposals.title: A Unified Percolation-Criticality Model for Electromagnetic Emission and Silence During Creep Fracturing in Coal and Shale
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 studies of coal and shale fracturing show complex electromagnetic (EM) signals, with bursts of emission and periods of silence, linked to gas disasters and reservoir stimulation. Existing models treat fracture dynamics and EM emission separately, lacking a framework that explains the transition between EM-active and EM-silent phases as a fundamental critical phenomenon. A unified theory is needed to predict these transitions, enhancing gas disaster early warning and the assessment of subsurface fracturing for carbon management.
proposals.hypothesis: The transition between EM emission and silence during creep fracturing is governed by a critical point in a coupled fracture-percolation network, where the correlation length of microcrack clusters diverges, and EM output follows scaling laws derived from critical phenomena theory.
proposals.method: We will integrate nanoindentation-derived time-dependent mechanical data (creep) with microscopic and fractal analysis of fracture networks from coal/shale samples. A 3D percolation lattice model will be developed, where bond-breaking probabilities are governed by a time-dependent creep law. The EM emission intensity will be modeled as a function of the rate of cluster coalescence near the percolation threshold. We will implement a Monte Carlo simulation to track the evolution of the fracture network and its associated EM output, calibrating it against laboratory acoustic/EM emission data from triaxial creep tests.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a first-principles model linking macroscopic EM silence/activation to microscopic fracture criticality, providing a predictive tool for hazard timing. It will establish scaling relationships between EM precursors and ultimate fracture, directly applicable to coal mine gas outburst prediction and the design of fracture networks for enhanced coalbed methane recovery or CO2 sequestration.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly modeling EM silence not as an absence of activity, but as a distinct phase in the critical dynamics of fracture percolation, integrating creep mechanics, fractal geometry, and critical phenomena into a single predictive framework for geophysical signals.

proposals.title: A Granular Micromechanics Model for MHz-kHz Electromagnetic Precursors During Fracturing of Coal and Shale
proposals.motivation: Electromagnetic (EM) emissions in the MHz-kHz range are observed during fracture processes in geomaterials and are proposed as potential precursors for coal and gas outbursts. However, the micro-mechanistic link between granular-scale damage, frictional slip, and the resulting transient charge separation (the tribo-/fracto-electrification effect) is poorly quantified. Establishing this link is critical for advancing predictive geophysical monitoring in coal mines and carbon storage sites, aligning with safety and carbon neutrality goals for the coal industry.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the amplitude and frequency spectrum of fracture-induced EM emissions can be quantitatively predicted by modeling the collective dynamics of micro-crack propagation and frictional sliding between mineral grains, where the charge generation rate is governed by the fractal statistics of newly created surface area and particle velocity.
proposals.method: We will develop a discrete element method (DEM) model, incorporating cohesive bonds and frictional contacts, to simulate the creep and fracturing of a granular assembly representing coal or shale microstructure. The model will track the time-dependent creation of new surface area (fractal dimension analysis) and the velocity of separating particles during each micro-fracture event. A coupled electrodynamic module will assign dipoles or charges proportional to these kinematic and geometric parameters, based on pre-2026 triboelectric laws, and sum their radiation to simulate the macroscopic EM signal. The model will be calibrated against existing nanoindentation creep data and lab-scale fracture-EM experiments.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first micromechanics-based, quantitative framework linking granular fracture dynamics to EM precursor signals. It will advance the fundamental understanding of fracture-induced electrification in heterogeneous geomaterials. The model will offer a new tool for interpreting field EM data to assess fracture network complexity and impending instability in coal reservoirs and caprocks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in integrating granular micromechanics (DEM) and fractal surface generation statistics directly with electrodynamic emission models, moving beyond empirical correlations to a physics-based, predictive approach for fracture EM precursors.

proposals.title: Linking Granular Flow Acoustic Emission to Electromagnetic Precursors: A Fractal-Based Sensing Framework for Micro-Fracture Monitoring in Coal and Shale
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, monitoring subsurface fracture evolution is critical for coalbed methane extraction, shale gas stimulation, and coal mine disaster prevention. Existing geophysical methods often lack the resolution to characterize the time-dependent micro-fracturing and granular particle flow that precede larger-scale failure. Techniques from granular physics, such as acoustic emission (AE) sensing and its correlation with electromagnetic (EM) signals during particle friction, offer an underexplored pathway for high-resolution, real-time monitoring.
proposals.hypothesis: The micro-fracturing and granular flow within coal and shale under creep conditions generate characteristic, coupled AE and EM signal patterns. These patterns can be quantitatively linked using fractal and statistical descriptors derived from granular flow sensing, providing a predictive precursor for macroscopic fracture network development.
proposals.method: We will conduct controlled laboratory creep experiments on coal and shale samples under triaxial stress, integrating high-frequency AE sensors and induction coil-based EM sensors. The time-series data will be analyzed using multi-fractal detrended fluctuation analysis (MF-DFA) to characterize the scale-invariant properties of both signal types. A cross-correlation and transfer entropy framework will be developed to establish causal links between AE energy release (granular flow) and EM pulse emissions (triboelectric effects). The microscopic fracture patterns post-experiment will be validated using scanning electron microscopy (SEM).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, multi-physics sensing methodology for the in-situ monitoring of micro-fracture processes. It will provide a mechanistic model linking granular dynamics to geophysical precursors, directly contributing to safer and more efficient gas extraction and disaster early-warning systems in coal and shale reservoirs.
proposals.novelty: The proposal uniquely transfers granular flow analysis techniques, specifically multi-fractal AE analysis, to the interpretation of fracture-induced EM signals—a correlation rarely quantified in geomechanics. It integrates Xie's expertise in microscopic time-dependent behavior and fracture networks with granular sensing, a crossover not yet fully explored in pre-2026 coal/shale research.

proposals.title: Fractal and Electromagnetic Precursors to Catastrophic Failure: A Coupled Fracture-EM Framework for Volcano and Landslide Early Warning
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, catastrophic geohazards like volcanic eruptions and large landslides remain notoriously difficult to predict with precision, often lacking reliable short-term precursors. Concurrently, research in coal and shale geomechanics has established strong links between micro-fracturing, time-dependent creep, and associated electromagnetic (EM) emissions. Scaling these laboratory and reservoir-scale findings to natural slope and volcanic systems could unlock new geophysical signals for early warning.
proposals.hypothesis: The accelerated, fractal progression of micro-fracturing during the tertiary creep phase of volcanic edifice or landslide destabilization generates a detectable, characteristic electromagnetic signature that precedes macroscopic failure.
proposals.method: We will integrate multi-scale monitoring data. First, we will analyze historical and newly acquired EM emission time-series from active volcanoes (e.g., Mt. Etna) and instrumented landslides, applying fractal dimension analysis (using box-counting and correlation dimension algorithms) to characterize the temporal clustering of EM events. Second, we will correlate these fractal EM signals with independent deformation data (InSAR, creep meters) and microseismicity. Finally, we will develop a coupled Discrete Element Method (DEM)-Finite Difference Time Domain (FDTD) numerical model to simulate fracture network evolution and the resultant EM emissions under realistic geo-stresses, calibrating it with the field data.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, physics-based link between geomechanical fracture processes and geophysical EM signals at the volcano/landslide scale. It will produce a quantitative framework for interpreting EM noise as a direct proxy for damage accumulation, moving beyond empirical correlation. The outcome will be a set of proposed fractal-EM metrics for integration into existing early-warning systems.
proposals.novelty: While fracture-EM studies exist for coal and engineered materials, the systematic application of fractal analysis to EM precursors, coupled with numerical modeling of the fracture-EM chain, for large-scale natural geohazards is novel. It uniquely bridges Heping Xie's expertise in time-dependent fracture of carbonaceous materials with volcanic and landslide monitoring.

proposals.title: Quantifying Time-Dependent Strength Recovery in Coal and Shale via Cyclic Nanoindentation with Inter-cycle Delays: Implications for Fracture Network Sustainability
proposals.motivation: In coalbed methane and shale gas extraction, hydraulic fracturing involves cyclic loading from injection pulses. Pre-2026 studies on creep and fracture in coal/shale focus on continuous damage, largely ignoring potential time-dependent healing between cycles. Understanding this recovery is crucial for designing injection schedules that maximize fracture network complexity while minimizing permanent damage to the reservoir's proppant-free fracture surfaces, directly impacting gas recovery efficiency and carbon-neutrality goals for coal industries.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that introducing time delays between cyclic nanoindentation loading cycles will induce measurable strength and stiffness recovery in coal and shale matrices due to viscoelastic crack closure and asperity re-bonding, and that this recovery follows a fractal scaling law dependent on the microstructure of the particles and organic matter.
proposals.method: We will employ high-temperature nanoindentation with a Berkovich tip to perform cyclic loading-unloading tests on polished coal and shale samples. Crucially, controlled time delays (from seconds to hours) will be introduced between cycles. The method integrates: 1) Continuous stiffness measurement (CSM) to track hardness and modulus evolution, 2) Analysis of unloading curve hysteresis to quantify energy dissipation and recovery, and 3) Post-indentation scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) to characterize residual imprint morphology and micro-crack networks. Fractal dimension analysis of crack patterns will be correlated with recovery metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative framework for strength recovery in unconventional reservoir rocks under cyclic subsurface conditions. It will deliver a phenomenological model linking recovery magnitude to delay time and material composition, offering practical insights for optimizing 'soaking' periods in multi-stage fracturing to enhance fracture network sustainability and gas production, thereby supporting the transition of the coal industry.
proposals.novelty: While cyclic nanoindentation is established, systematically varying inter-cycle delay times to isolate healing effects in coal/shale is novel. Combining this with fractal analysis of recovered micro-crack networks moves beyond traditional continuum damage models to a multi-scale, time-aware characterization of mechanical behavior.

proposals.title: A Fractal-Based Constitutive Model for the S-Shaped Damage Evolution in Coal and Shale Under Long-Term Creep and Fracturing
proposals.motivation: Understanding time-dependent damage evolution in geomaterials like coal and shale is critical for predicting long-term stability in carbon storage, coalbed methane extraction, and underground engineering. Pre-2026 experimental data, including nanoindentation creep tests, often reveals an S-shaped (sigmoidal) progression of damage, but existing constitutive models frequently fail to capture this non-linear transition from incubation to acceleration to stabilization. A theoretical model grounded in micromechanics is needed to bridge this gap and inform safer, more efficient subsurface operations.
proposals.hypothesis: The S-shaped damage evolution curve can be theoretically derived by coupling a fractal description of microcrack network growth with a time-dependent, energy-based damage factor, where the sigmoidal shape emerges from the interaction between crack incubation, coalescence, and stress redistribution.
proposals.method: The research will integrate fractal geometry and continuum damage mechanics. First, we will analyze nanoindentation creep data from shale and coal to characterize the S-shaped damage factor evolution. A fractal damage variable, dependent on the evolving microcrack size distribution and density, will be defined. This variable will be embedded into a viscoelastic-plastic constitutive framework, where the kinetic equation for damage growth is formulated as a non-linear differential equation driven by the energy release rate. The model parameters will be calibrated against experimental creep-fracture data using inverse analysis and optimization algorithms.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new constitutive model that quantitatively predicts the full S-shaped damage trajectory in brittle geomaterials, enhancing the accuracy of long-term deformation and failure forecasts. It will directly contribute to improved designs for hydraulic fracturing in coal reservoirs and risk assessment for subsurface structures under carbon neutrality transition pressures.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit mechanistic derivation of the S-shaped curve from fractal micromechanics, moving beyond empirical fitting, and in the application of this integrated approach to the coupled creep-fracturing processes prevalent in coal and shale energy systems.

proposals.title: Bridging Scales in Coal Pillar Stability: Validating Laboratory-Derived Energy Damage Factors with In-Situ Monitoring Data
proposals.motivation: The transition to carbon neutrality pressures China's coal industry to enhance safety and resource recovery efficiency. Pillar stability is critical for safe mining and gas extraction, but a persistent scale gap exists between laboratory-derived damage models and field-scale pillar behavior. Validating lab-scale creep and fracturing models with real-world data is essential for reliable pillar design and disaster reduction.
proposals.hypothesis: The energy-based damage factors derived from laboratory nanoindentation and creep tests on coal particles exhibit a scalable, fractal relationship with the macroscopic damage evolution captured by in-situ monitoring of mining pillars under long-term load.
proposals.method: The research will integrate pre-2026 methods: 1) Laboratory-scale: Conduct nanoindentation creep tests on coal samples to derive time-dependent mechanical properties and calculate an energy damage factor (D) using methods from Xie's prior shale work. Fractal analysis will characterize the micro-fracture networks. 2) Field-scale: Analyze existing in-situ monitoring data (e.g., microseismic, stress, deformation) from case study coal pillars. Extract a field-scale damage indicator from energy release rates of recorded seismic events. 3) Validation: Employ scaling laws and statistical correlation algorithms to compare the temporal evolution and magnitude of lab-derived D with the field indicator, testing for fractal scaling relationships.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, quantitative link between microscale damage mechanisms and pillar-scale stability, directly enhancing predictive models for coal pillar creep and failure. It will deliver a validated methodology for upscaling laboratory results to field engineering design, contributing to improved gas extraction strategies and coal mine disaster reduction.
proposals.novelty: While lab studies and field monitoring exist in parallel, this proposal directly correlates a lab-quantified energy damage factor with in-situ seismic energy release, using fractal theory to bridge the scale gap—a non-obvious integration of Xie's expertise in nanoindentation, fractals, and field-scale fracturing.

proposals.title: Full-Field Strain Localization and Energy Dissipation in Coal Under Creep-Fracturing Conditions Using Digital Image Correlation
proposals.motivation: Under the dual pressures of carbon neutrality goals and the need for subsurface coal gas extraction, understanding the time-dependent, heterogeneous mechanical behavior of coal is critical for predicting long-term reservoir stability and fracture network evolution. Pre-2026 nanoindentation studies on shale and macroscopic fracturing tests on coal lack the mesoscale, full-field observation of strain localization and energy dissipation mechanisms that govern progressive failure and gas flow pathways.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the creep deformation and fracturing of coal, under confining pressure and gas adsorption, will localize strain and dissipate energy in distinct, fractal patterns that correlate with microstructural heterogeneity and can be quantitatively linked to macroscopic strength degradation.
proposals.method: We will employ a coupled experimental-numerical approach. Coal specimens, prepared with a speckle pattern, will undergo triaxial creep and stepwise fracturing tests with concurrent gas (CO2/CH4) injection. A stereo-DIC system (e.g., Correlated Solutions' VIC-3D) will capture full-field, time-resolved 3D displacement and strain maps. The heterogeneous strain fields will be analyzed using fractal dimension algorithms (e.g., box-counting method on strain contour maps) and correlated with post-test microscopic SEM imaging of fracture networks and particle distributions. Energy dissipation zones will be localized by integrating DIC-derived strain fields with synchronized load data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, full-field maps linking creep-induced strain localization to fracture initiation and propagation in coal under gas environments. It will establish a mesoscale bridge between microscopic particle behavior and reservoir-scale fracturing, offering a new methodology to validate and improve constitutive models for coal seam stability and enhanced coalbed methane recovery.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct, experimental quantification of energy dissipation zones via DIC during coal creep-fracturing, combined with a fractal analysis of strain localization patterns—a synthesis not previously applied to coal under carbon-constrained engineering conditions.

proposals.title: Fatigue Life Prediction of Coal and Shale Under Cyclic Loading: Adapting Metallurgical S-N and Fracture Mechanics Models
proposals.motivation: The transition to carbon neutrality necessitates enhanced coalbed methane extraction and geological carbon storage, processes involving cyclic fluid injection that induces fatigue damage in coal and shale. Pre-2026 rock mechanics lacks robust, predictive models for the long-term, time-dependent fracturing and creep behavior of these geomaterials under such cyclic stress, which is critical for optimizing stimulation and ensuring reservoir integrity.
proposals.hypothesis: The fatigue life and subcritical crack growth in heterogeneous, porous coal and shale under cyclic loading can be quantitatively predicted by adapting metallurgical fatigue models (e.g., Basquin's law for S-N curves, Paris' law for crack growth), provided the models are modified to account for the materials' intrinsic fractal fracture networks and viscoelastic creep properties.
proposals.method: We will conduct cyclic nanoindentation and uniaxial compression tests on coal and shale samples to generate fatigue life (cycles-to-failure) data across stress amplitudes. The S-N curve approach (Basquin's law: σ_a = σ_f' * (2N_f)^b) will be fitted, with parameters linked to sample mineralogy and porosity via microscopic SEM-EDS analysis. For crack growth, we will use pre-fatigued samples and in-situ microscopic observation to measure crack length per cycle, adapting Paris' law (da/dN = C(ΔK)^m) by incorporating a time-dependent term derived from creep compliance tests. Fractal dimension analysis of fracture surfaces will correlate with fatigue parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative framework for predicting fatigue-driven damage evolution in coal and shale, directly applicable to designing cyclic injection protocols for gas recovery and CO2 storage. It will establish a novel link between metallurgical durability science and geomechanics, offering a methodology to assess the long-term stability of subsurface energy reservoirs.
proposals.novelty: While fatigue models are mature in metallurgy, their direct adaptation to complex, creeping geomaterials like coal and shale—integrating fractal fracture characteristics and time-dependent deformation—is a novel, non-trivial cross-disciplinary advancement beyond existing static or monotonic loading rock fracture studies.

proposals.title: Quantifying Creep Damage and Fracture Evolution in Coal and Shale via Thermodynamic Entropy Production: A Coupled Nanoindentation and Fractal Analysis
proposals.motivation: The long-term stability of coal and shale reservoirs under stress is critical for safe mining, carbon sequestration, and unconventional gas extraction, yet predicting time-dependent damage and fracture network evolution remains a challenge. Pre-2026 research on creep and fracturing often relies on phenomenological models that lack a fundamental thermodynamic basis linking microscopic mechanisms to macroscopic failure. A framework grounded in non-equilibrium thermodynamics could provide a more universal metric for damage accumulation.
proposals.hypothesis: The rate of entropy production, derived from dissipative processes during nanoindentation creep tests, serves as a fundamental and predictive internal variable for damage state, correlating directly with the fractal dimension of induced fracture networks in coal and shale.
proposals.method: We will conduct nanoindentation creep tests on coal and shale samples, measuring time-dependent displacement under constant load. The dissipated energy will be calculated from the hysteresis of load-displacement curves. The entropy production rate will be computed using established thermodynamic frameworks (e.g., following Ziegler's principle or using the Clausius-Duhem inequality). Concurrently, we will perform triaxial compression tests with acoustic emission monitoring to generate fracture networks. The fractal dimension of these networks will be quantified from post-test CT scans using box-counting algorithms. A correlation model will be developed to link the microscale entropy production rate to the macroscale fractal fracture dimension.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish entropy production as a novel, thermodynamics-based damage metric for geomaterials, moving beyond empirical parameters. It will provide a quantitative bridge between microscopic time-dependent deformation (creep) and the complexity of macroscopic fracture networks, offering a new theoretical tool for predicting long-term reservoir integrity and optimizing stimulation designs for coalbed methane and shale gas.
proposals.novelty: While entropy has been discussed in rock mechanics, its direct application as a *rate-based* internal variable for damage, explicitly coupled with experimental nanoindentation data and fractal fracture analysis for coal/shale systems, is a novel synthesis of thermodynamics, experimental micromechanics, and geometric characterization.

proposals.title: Grain-Scale Discrete Element Modeling of Sandstone Under Cyclic Loading: Energy Partitioning and Fractal Damage Evolution
proposals.motivation: Understanding the time-dependent, cyclic mechanical behavior of reservoir rocks like sandstone is critical for predicting the long-term stability of geological carbon storage sites and the efficiency of cyclic stimulation in enhanced gas recovery. Pre-2026, while macroscopic cyclic tests exist, the grain-scale mechanisms governing energy dissipation, microcrack evolution, and the transition from distributed damage to macroscopic fracture remain poorly quantified, limiting predictive models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that under cyclic loading, the dissipated energy in a sandstone DEM model partitions into distinct, quantifiable ratios between frictional sliding, bond breakage, and kinetic energy, and that the evolution of microcrack networks follows a fractal scaling law that predicts the onset of macroscopic failure.
proposals.method: We will construct a high-fidelity 3D Discrete Element Method (DEM) model of sandstone using a bonded-particle approach with clumped particles to represent realistic grain shapes and mineralogy (e.g., quartz, feldspar). The model will be calibrated against pre-2026 nanoindentation and triaxial test data. Cyclic loading protocols will be implemented using a servo-control algorithm. Energy components (strain energy, frictional dissipation, bond breakage energy) will be tracked per computational cycle. Post-processing will involve analyzing the evolving contact force chains and microcrack networks using fractal dimension analysis (e.g., box-counting method) to characterize damage progression.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a first-principles, grain-scale framework for quantifying energy partitioning during cyclic deformation of sandstones, directly linking micro-mechanical processes to macroscopic fatigue laws. It will deliver a predictive model for damage accumulation and a microstructurally-informed fractal damage parameter usable in larger-scale continuum models for reservoir engineering.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit integration of cyclic energy budgeting with the fractal analysis of evolving grain-scale damage within a DEM framework, moving beyond static strength or single-cycle simulations to model the fundamental processes of time-dependent, cyclic fatigue in porous rocks.

proposals.title: A Multi-Scale Energy Damage Framework for Long-Term Coal Pillar Stability Assessment Under Carbon-Constrained Mining Conditions
proposals.motivation: The long-term stability of coal pillars is critical for safe mining and post-mining land use, especially as China's carbon neutrality goals accelerate mine closure and repurposing. Traditional pillar design, based on empirical strength scaling laws, inadequately captures time-dependent creep failure and the complex damage accumulation from repeated fracturing events. A physics-based, multi-scale failure criterion is urgently needed to assess pillar integrity over decadal timescales.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the energy damage thresholds derived from nanoindentation creep tests on coal and shale particles can be scaled up through fractal principles to serve as a constitutive failure criterion in continuum models, accurately predicting the time-dependent, large-scale failure of mine pillars.
proposals.method: First, we will conduct nanoindentation creep tests on coal and associated shale samples to quantify time-dependent mechanical properties and extract microscopic energy damage thresholds. Second, using fractal geometry, we will develop scaling laws to relate these micro-scale energy parameters to meso-scale fracture network properties observed in lab compression tests. Finally, we will implement these scaled energy-damage criteria as user-defined material models in a 3D finite-difference code (e.g., FLAC3D) to simulate the long-term deformation and stability of representative coal pillar systems under varying loading and constraint conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, mechanics-based methodology for pillar stability assessment that integrates micro-scale creep behavior with macro-scale failure. It will deliver a practical numerical modeling framework for evaluating long-term pillar performance, directly informing strategies for gas disaster reduction in active mines and stability management for abandoned mines in the context of carbon neutrality.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct upscaling of lab-derived, time-dependent energy damage parameters—a micro-fracture process metric—to serve as a failure criterion in large-scale geomechanical models, moving beyond conventional strength-based or purely phenomenological creep laws.

proposals.title: Unloading-Induced Creep Damage and Fractal Evolution in Coal: A Coupled Nanoindentation and Microscopic Fracture Analysis
proposals.motivation: In coal mining and gas extraction, cyclic loading/unloading induces damage, yet research predominantly focuses on loading phases. The unloading phase, critical for understanding long-term stability and creep behavior in carbon-containing geomaterials, is poorly characterized. This gap limits our ability to predict delayed failure and optimize engineering practices for safety and carbon neutrality goals.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the unloading phase in cyclic stress regimes induces distinct, time-dependent (creep) damage mechanisms in coal, characterized by a unique fractal evolution of micro-fractures and particle detachment, which differs fundamentally from damage accrued during loading.
proposals.method: We will employ cyclic nanoindentation with extended hold periods during unloading to quantify time-dependent deformation. Post-indentation, microscopic (SEM/AFM) and fractal analysis (using box-counting and particle analysis algorithms) will map the surface and subsurface fracture networks. This will be correlated with acoustic emission data from larger-scale cyclic triaxial tests on coal samples to bridge nano/micro-scale damage with macro-scale behavior.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a mechanistic model for unloading damage in coal, linking microscopic creep processes to macroscopic fracture network development. It will provide a novel damage parameter for engineering design, improving predictions of long-term wellbore stability and fracture complexity in coalbed methane reservoirs, thereby contributing to safer extraction and carbon management.
proposals.novelty: While creep and fracturing are studied separately, this proposal uniquely targets the unloading phase as a primary driver of time-dependent damage, integrating nanoindentation-based creep measurement with fractal analysis of resultant fracture patterns—a combination not yet applied to this specific problem in coal mechanics.

proposals.title: Inferring Cyclic Loading History in Coal and Shale from Ultrasonic and Energy Signatures: An Inverse Fractal-Damage Modeling Approach
proposals.motivation: Understanding the past stress history of geological formations like coal and shale is critical for assessing long-term wellbore integrity, predicting delayed gas outbursts, and evaluating caprock stability for carbon storage. Current methods primarily predict future damage from known loads, but the inverse problem—deducing past loading from present damage—remains a significant challenge, limiting forensic analysis of field failures and long-term risk assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: The complex damage state in coal and shale, characterized by ultrasonic wave attenuation and dissipated energy density, encodes a unique signature of its prior cyclic loading history (amplitude, frequency, cycles) that can be decoded using a physics-informed inverse model coupling fractal fracture mechanics with machine learning.
proposals.method: We will first conduct cyclic loading-unloading experiments on coal and shale samples, simultaneously monitoring ultrasonic P-wave/S-wave velocities, attenuation, and hysteretic energy dissipation. The resulting damage will be quantified microscopically (SEM) and described using fractal dimensions of fracture networks. A forward model will be built using a fractional derivative-based creep-damage constitutive law to simulate damage accumulation under various loading histories. The inverse problem will be solved using a Bayesian optimization framework, where the forward model is embedded within a surrogate model (Gaussian Process) to efficiently search the loading parameter space (amplitude, frequency, cycle count) that best matches the measured ultrasonic and energy signatures.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, quantitative methodology for the forensic analysis of in-situ rock stress history, directly applicable to post-mortem analysis of wellbore instability and gas outburst sites. It will advance the fundamental understanding of the relationship between time-dependent damage metrics and loading paths in heterogeneous carbonaceous materials, providing a new tool for validating long-term geomechanical models used in carbon sequestration and coal mine disaster reduction.
proposals.novelty: While forward damage modeling is well-established, this research uniquely inverts the process by fusing fractal characterization of real damage with a physics-informed machine learning algorithm to decode loading history, moving beyond simple pattern recognition to a mechanistic solution of the inverse problem for geological materials.

proposals.title: Decoupling Strength and Permeability in Thermally Fractured Coal: A Microscopic and Fractal Analysis of Creep and Flow Enhancement
proposals.motivation: In the context of carbon-neutrality goals and the need for efficient coalbed methane (CBM) extraction and CO₂ geo-sequestration, enhancing reservoir permeability is critical. Thermal fracturing is a recognized stimulation technique, but its application is constrained by the prevailing assumption that induced cracks inevitably and proportionally degrade the macroscopic shear strength of the coal mass, risking wellbore instability and subsidence. This project challenges that assumption to optimize stimulation strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: Thermally induced micro-cracks in coal can create a preferential, fractal flow network that significantly increases macroscopic permeability while having a non-proportional, minimal impact on the long-term shear strength, due to the time-dependent (creep) re-compaction and healing of non-critical crack surfaces under confining stress.
proposals.method: We will conduct controlled thermal treatment on coal samples, followed by integrated microscopic and macroscopic testing. Nanoindentation creep tests will quantify the time-dependent mechanical properties (hardness, modulus, creep compliance) of the coal matrix and crack tips. X-ray micro-CT and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) will characterize the 3D crack network morphology. Fractal dimension analysis (using box-counting algorithms on binarized CT images) will quantify crack complexity. Concurrently, triaxial shear and permeability tests under simulated reservoir stress will measure the coupled macroscopic strength and flow properties. A correlation model linking fractal crack parameters, nanoindentation creep data, and macro-scale performance will be developed.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic, quantitative framework for evaluating thermal stimulation that moves beyond the simple strength-reduction paradigm. It will deliver practical criteria for designing thermal treatments in CBM and CO₂ storage projects to maximize permeability gain while maintaining geomechanical integrity. The findings will directly inform gas disaster reduction strategies by offering a controlled fracturing method.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly testing the decoupling of permeability and strength, a non-obvious outcome, by integrating pre-2026 methods of nanoindentation creep analysis with fractal quantification of thermal cracks. It uniquely focuses on the role of time-dependent coal behavior in mitigating strength loss from induced fractures.

proposals.title: Harnessing Catastrophic Failure in Quartz/Feldspar for Enhanced Fracture Network Generation in Coalbed Methane Reservoirs: A Microscopic and Fractal Analysis
proposals.motivation: In the context of China's carbon neutrality goals, enhancing coalbed methane (CBM) recovery is critical for both energy security and gas disaster reduction. Traditional hydraulic fracturing in coal reservoirs aims for controlled, progressive fracture growth, but often yields suboptimal network complexity. This research challenges the prevailing view that catastrophic brittle failure in hard mineral inclusions is detrimental, proposing it may instead be a key, rapid mechanism for generating complex fracture pathways.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the catastrophic, high-energy brittle failure of quartz and feldspar particles embedded within a coal or shale matrix can act as a beneficial, rapid nucleation mechanism for fracture networks, enhancing overall permeability more effectively than progressive failure alone under specific geomechanical conditions.
proposals.method: We will employ nanoindentation creep tests on polished shale and coal samples to characterize the time-dependent and catastrophic failure behavior of quartz/feldspar inclusions versus the organic/clay matrix. High-resolution scanning electron microscopy (SEM) will image the resulting fracture morphologies. Fractal dimension analysis (using the box-counting algorithm) will quantify the complexity of fracture networks initiated from catastrophic versus progressive failure sites. These microscopic results will be integrated with discrete element method (DEM) simulations to upscale the effect of catastrophic failure events on reservoir-scale fracture network generation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel mechanistic framework for fracture initiation in heterogeneous geomaterials, directly linking microscopic failure modes to macroscopic network properties. It will offer practical insights for designing stimulation strategies (e.g., pulse injection) that leverage brittle failure of minerals to rapidly enhance fracture complexity in CBM and shale gas reservoirs, supporting both gas extraction and coal mine safety.
proposals.novelty: The proposal inverts the conventional engineering paradigm that seeks to suppress catastrophic failure, instead investigating its potential utility. It uniquely combines nanoindentation-based creep/fracture studies with fractal analysis to quantitatively bridge the gap between mineral-scale brittle mechanics and reservoir-scale fracture network generation.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Fractal and Time-Dependent Deflection of Cracks at Heterogeneous Coal-Shale Interfaces: An Integrated Nanoindentation and Microscopic Fracture Mechanics Study
proposals.motivation: The efficiency of hydraulic fracturing for coalbed methane extraction and CO₂ geo-sequestration in coal depends on creating complex fracture networks. Crack propagation paths are critically controlled by deflection at mineral interfaces within heterogeneous coal and shale, but a systematic, quantitative framework linking interface properties to crack trajectory is lacking. This gap hinders the predictive design of fracturing strategies to enhance permeability while managing long-term creep-induced fracture evolution.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the deflection angle and branching probability of a propagating crack at a bi-mineralic interface are governed by a non-dimensional parameter combining interface orientation, strength (characterized by nanoindentation), and the time-dependent viscoelastic properties of the adjoining materials.
proposals.method: We will fabricate controlled composite samples pairing coal with common shale minerals (e.g., calcite, clay). Using a high-precision nanoindentation system with a cube-corner tip, we will induce controlled micro-cracks and measure the time-dependent (creep) mechanical properties of individual phases and their interfaces. In-situ microscopic fracture experiments under a scanning electron microscope (SEM) will track real-time crack paths. Crack trajectories and surface roughness will be analyzed using fractal dimension (box-counting algorithm) and deflection angle quantification via digital image correlation (DIC). Statistical analysis will correlate these fracture metrics with the measured mechanical contrast and interface orientation.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a quantitative, mechanics-based rule set for crack deflection at heterogeneous interfaces, directly informing hydraulic fracturing models for coal reservoirs. It will provide novel data on the time-dependent fracture behavior at interfaces, crucial for predicting long-term seal integrity in carbon storage. The findings will bridge microscopic fracture mechanics with macroscopic reservoir engineering.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integrated, quantitative study of time-dependent (creep) mechanical properties and real-time crack propagation at defined mineral interfaces, combining nanoindentation creep tests with in-situ SEM fracturing and fractal analysis of crack paths—a synthesis not yet fully explored in pre-2026 coal/shale fracture mechanics.

proposals.title: Inverse Reconstruction of Thermal History in Coal and Shale via Fractal Analysis of Thermally-Induced Micro-Crack Networks
proposals.motivation: Understanding the thermal history of geological formations is critical for assessing coalbed methane reservoir evolution, underground coal gasification efficiency, and the long-term integrity of carbon storage sites. Current methods for inferring thermal history (e.g., vitrinite reflectance, apatite fission tracks) are indirect or require specific minerals. A direct method linking observable micro-damage to thermal cycling parameters would provide a powerful new tool for subsurface characterization, especially relevant for China's energy transition and carbon neutrality goals impacting its coal industry.
proposals.hypothesis: The fractal dimension, spatial density, and morphological characteristics of micro-crack networks in coal and shale matrices are unique functions of the number and peak intensity of past thermal cycles, enabling the quantitative inversion of thermal history from microscopic observations.
proposals.method: We will conduct controlled laboratory thermal cycling experiments on coal and shale samples, using furnaces to apply known thermal histories. The resulting micro-crack patterns will be characterized using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM), quantifying parameters like crack length, aperture, orientation, and connectivity. Fractal analysis (box-counting and multifractal spectrum methods) will be applied to digitized crack networks. An inverse model will be developed using a Bayesian framework, where a forward model (based on thermo-elastic stress and damage mechanics) is coupled with Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling to infer the most probable thermal history parameters from observed crack patterns.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, microstructure-based diagnostic method for paleo-thermal reconstruction in sedimentary rocks. It will provide quantitative links between thermal loading, time-dependent creep damage, and fracture network complexity, advancing the fundamental understanding of thermal cracking in heterogeneous geomaterials. The methodology will offer a new tool for evaluating the thermal stability of coal reservoirs and caprocks for carbon sequestration.
proposals.novelty: While fractal analysis of fractures is established, its application to solve the inverse problem of thermal history reconstruction from crack patterns in coal/shale is novel. The integration of controlled experiments, fractal metrics, and Bayesian inversion specifically for thermal cycles in carbonaceous rocks represents a significant departure from existing geothermometry techniques.

proposals.title: Designing Tailored Geocomposites via Inverse Analysis of Mineral Failure Modes: A Fractal and Nanoindentation Approach
proposals.motivation: The transition to a carbon-neutral economy requires engineered geo-materials for applications like CO2 storage, geothermal energy, and coal mine disaster reduction, where controlling shear failure is critical. Current composite design is largely empirical, lacking a fundamental link between the microscopic failure mechanisms of constituent minerals and the macroscopic shear resistance of the synthesized material. A systematic, inverse design framework based on failure insights is needed.
proposals.hypothesis: The macroscopic shear strength and creep resistance of a synthetic rock composite can be predictively tailored by inversely designing its microstructure based on the fractal characteristics and time-dependent failure modes of its mineralogical components.
proposals.method: We will first characterize the nanoindentation creep response and post-failure fractal dimensions of fracture surfaces for key minerals (e.g., calcite, quartz, clay) and coal particles. Using this database, we will develop an inverse design algorithm that employs a genetic algorithm to optimize the composition, particle size distribution (modeled with fractal scaling), and bonding matrix to achieve a target shear strength profile. Synthetic composites will be fabricated via pressing and sintering, and their mechanical performance will be validated through direct shear tests coupled with microscopic (SEM) analysis of induced fracture networks.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, mechanics-informed methodology for designing geocomposites, moving beyond trial-and-error. It will provide a quantitative framework linking mineral-scale failure to bulk shear behavior, with direct applications in designing proppants for complex fracture networks or engineered barriers for subsurface containment.
proposals.novelty: The approach inverts the traditional failure analysis paradigm, using knowledge of how things break to design how they should be built. It uniquely integrates nanoindentation-derived creep parameters and fractal fracture geometry as direct inputs into a computational composite optimization model.

ideas.title: A multi-fidelity framework combining quantum mechanics, machine learning, and molecular dynamics to predict BNT mechanical properties and failure mechanisms
ideas.description: This research overcomes force field inaccuracy by establishing a closed-loop, multi-scale simulation framework. High-throughput classical MD identifies potential failure modes, which are then recalculated using precise DFT to generate a benchmark dataset. A machine-learned interatomic potential is trained on this data, enabling large-scale simulations with near-DFT accuracy. The final output is a validated, high-accuracy model for BNT strength, flexibility, and fracture that explicitly quantifies and bridges scale-dependent uncertainties.

ideas.title: Adapting Civil Engineering's Life-Cycle Cost Analysis to Thermodynamic System Design
ideas.description: This research adapts the Life-Cycle Cost Analysis (LCCA) paradigm from civil engineering to challenge the assumption that thermodynamic efficiency is the primary design goal. In civil engineering, moving from minimizing initial construction cost to minimizing total lifetime cost revolutionized material and design choices (e.g., using more expensive, durable concrete to avoid frequent repairs). The analogous solution is to apply a formal LCCA framework to thermodynamic systems. The adapted idea is to develop a multi-variable cost function for system design that integrates capital costs, operational energy expenses, maintenance schedules, working fluid degradation and replacement costs, environmental penalty costs (e.g., for GWP or toxicity), and end-of-life disposal/recycling expenses. The optimization target becomes the net present value of this aggregated lifetime cost, not peak efficiency. This would systematically justify selecting fluids or components with lower efficiency but superior longevity, stability, or environmental profile, ultimately leading to designs with a lower true total cost of ownership.

ideas.title: Adapting Seismic Signal Analysis for ORC Expander Fault Diagnosis
ideas.description: This idea adapts fault-diagnosis methods from geophysics to ORC expanders. In seismology, arrays of sensors analyze propagating elastic waves to locate and classify seismic events. The analogous solution uses advanced signal processing to differentiate event types based on acoustic signatures. Adapted to the ORC context, this involves deploying a sensor array on the expander to monitor internal pressure waves and vibrations. By applying adapted seismic analysis techniques, such as waveform correlation and spectral pattern recognition, the system can classify specific failure modes in the expander, providing early warning for maintenance, much like early earthquake detection systems.

ideas.title: System-Coupled Informatics for Co-Design of ORC Fluids and Components
ideas.description: This approach extends materials informatics by embedding the search for novel working fluids within a holistic system co-design framework. It employs a concurrent screening pipeline that evaluates not just thermodynamic properties but also chemical stability, material compatibility, environmental impact, and cost at the earliest stages. The core advance is the integration of these molecular-scale filters with system-level performance optimization, using reduced-order models to co-adapt turbine expansion and heat exchanger design parameters with the fluid's characteristics. This ensures the final candidates are optimized for real-world performance, manufacturability, and economic viability, transforming a pure discovery tool into a practical engineering design system.

ideas.title: Investigate boron nanotube stability via strain field mapping and defect engineering
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from structural engineering for large-span shells to the nanoscale. In macro-scale domes, stability at larger spans is ensured by analyzing strain fields and engineering controlled imperfections or reinforcements. Analogously, the project will first map the evolving strain and stress fields in boron nanotubes as their diameter increases beyond previously studied limits to identify the precise instability mechanisms. Then, it will computationally design and evaluate specific atomic-scale defect patterns within the BNT lattice, treating them not as flaws but as engineered 'nanoscale stiffeners,' to enhance stability for larger diameters, thereby extending the viable size range of BNTs.

ideas.title: Micro-scale ORC with biomimetic capillary-driven working fluid transport
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from plant physiology—specifically the capillary-driven transport of water in xylem vessels—to address the critical challenge of working fluid circulation in micro-scale Organic Rankine Cycle (ORC) systems for electronics waste heat recovery. The analogous problem of moving fluid with minimal energy input in biological systems is solved via passive capillary action and surface tension within micro-porous structures. The adapted idea replaces traditional mechanical micropumps with a biomimetic, porous wick evaporator design. This passive capillary system uses the waste heat from components like server chips to drive the phase change and subsequent capillary pull of the organic fluid, enabling a self-circulating, ultra-compact ORC loop that recovers waste heat without consuming parasitic power for pumping, thereby maximizing net power gain and scalability down to the chip level.

ideas.title: High-throughput discovery of non-ideal nanotube sorbents for adsorption-driven thermal energy conversion
ideas.description: This research direction applies high-throughput computational screening and machine learning methods, inspired by drug and fluid discovery, to systematically explore the property landscape of nanotubes derived from strained or reconstructed 2D sheets (like boron allotropes). Instead of screening for ideal molecular working fluids, the target is identifying optimal nanotube sorbents whose non-ideal, tunable pore geometries and strain-induced electronic states maximize performance metrics for adsorption-driven thermal cycles (e.g., for waste heat recovery). The synergy enables the inverse design of nanotube structures—bypassing the 'perfect rolling' assumption—to precisely match the adsorption characteristics required for efficient, novel thermodynamic cycles, merging nanomaterial synthesis with system-level energy conversion design.

ideas.title: Adapting Catalyst Discovery Pipelines for ORC Working Fluid Screening
ideas.description: This idea adapts the high-throughput virtual screening (HTVS) pipeline methodology from the field of heterogeneous catalysis to the discovery of novel Organic Rankine Cycle (ORC) working fluids. In catalysis, HTVS combines first-principles calculations (e.g., DFT for adsorption energies) with machine learning to screen millions of material compositions. The adapted solution configures a similar computational pipeline to screen molecular databases for candidate fluids. It would use quantum chemistry and molecular dynamics to calculate target properties like critical parameters, toxicity, and environmental impact. A machine learning model trained on this generated data would then act as a fast surrogate predictor, enabling the efficient exploration of a vast chemical design space to identify high-performance, environmentally benign working fluid candidates for specific ORC applications.

ideas.title: High-Efficiency, Deployable Waste Heat Recovery Using Boron Nanotube-Enhanced ORC Modules
ideas.description: This research direction develops modular, containerized ORC units specifically engineered with boron nanotube-based components. The core investigation focuses on leveraging the predicted high thermal conductivity and strength-to-weight ratio of large-diameter BNTs to create next-generation heat exchanger surfaces and durable, lightweight membranes for working fluid management. By integrating these advanced materials, the modules achieve a significant leap in power density and cycle efficiency, allowing a single standardized unit to harvest energy from lower-temperature or more variable waste streams than current mobile ORC systems. This enables rapid, cost-effective deployment for temporary industrial sites, remote mining operations, or disaster recovery, where high-performance, compact waste heat recovery was previously infeasible.

ideas.title: Boron Nanotube-Enhanced Adsorption Cooling via Reverse ORC for Ultra-Stable Waste Heat Recovery
ideas.description: This research investigates the integration of boron nanotubes (BNTs) as a next-generation adsorbent into a reverse ORC cycle for adsorption cooling. The core synergy lies in utilizing the predicted stability and tunable properties of large-diameter BNTs to create an adsorbent bed with superior thermal conductivity, structural integrity, and adsorption capacity for the ORC working fluid. This addresses key limitations of traditional adsorbents, such as poor heat transfer and degradation, enabling a more efficient, compact, and precisely controlled cooling system. The work will computationally model the stability and adsorption isotherms of large-diameter BNTs to design an optimized, ultra-stable adsorption bed that significantly enhances the performance and lifespan of waste-heat-driven cooling cycles.

ideas.title: Atomic-scale characterization of thermoelectric BNTs for cost-optimized ORC waste heat recovery
ideas.description: This research fuses advanced materials characterization with thermal system engineering to develop novel, cost-effective Organic Rankine Cycle (ORC) components. The core idea is to synthesize and functionalize boron nitride nanotubes (BNTs) as high-surface-area, corrosion-resistant coatings for ORC heat exchangers, using scanning tunneling microscopy (STM) to directly correlate atomic-scale electronic structure with macroscopic thermoelectric and heat transfer performance. This atomic-level insight allows for the targeted optimization of BNT synthesis to maximize the coefficient of performance (COP) of the heat exchange process. The result is a materials-informed design pathway for building-scale ORC systems that simultaneously reduces capital cost through enhanced efficiency and durable, tailored materials.

ideas.title: Apply photonic crystal inverse design to ORC heat exchanger micro-architecture
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to a parallel problem in photonics—designing optimal nanostructures via inverse design and adjoint-based topology optimization—to the context of ORC heat exchanger design. In photonics, this method computationally discovers highly efficient, non-intuitive geometries for target light manipulation by directly optimizing material distribution for a desired electromagnetic field response. The adapted research idea applies this paradigm to the thermal-fluid domain, using a similar computational framework to optimize the microscale solid material layout within a heat exchanger's repeating unit cell. The objective is to maximize a combined performance metric (e.g., heat transfer per unit pumping power) for ORC fluids, generating novel, high-performance, and potentially lightweight porous micro-architectures that would be difficult to conceive through conventional design approaches.

ideas.title: Use materials informatics for working fluid discovery

ideas.title: In-situ acoustic and vibrational spectroscopy for defect-induced property transitions in boron nanotubes
ideas.description: This research fuses the investigation of defect-induced semiconducting behavior in boron nanotubes with advanced vibration/acoustic fault-diagnosis methods from aerospace. The core idea is to employ high-sensitivity acoustic emission and vibrational spectroscopy techniques as in-situ probes during the synthesis or post-processing of boron nanotubes. By analyzing the unique vibrational signatures (phonon spectra) and acoustic emissions caused by defect formation or doping, we can directly correlate specific structural modifications with emergent semiconducting electronic properties measured simultaneously. This enables a closed-loop, non-destructive methodology for actively engineering and validating the metallic-to-semiconducting transition in boron nanotubes, while also providing a predictive diagnostic framework for nanoscale material 'failure' or property degradation under operational stress.

ideas.title: Ultra-low-cost boron nanocluster fluids for non-equilibrium thermal management
ideas.description: This research direction inverts the typical materials science priority by targeting the synthesis of boron nanoclusters and nanoribbons specifically optimized for minimal total lifetime cost when dispersed as nanofluids in industrial thermal systems. The core investigation focuses on how the size, edge termination, and surface functionalization of finite boron structures influence not just thermal properties, but, more critically, the key cost drivers: synthetic scalability, colloidal stability without expensive surfactants, corrosion inhibition, and long-term recyclability. The goal is to discover boron cluster formulations where the marginal performance gain over a cheap base fluid justifies its cost by significantly reducing pumping power, maintenance, or system size over a decades-long lifespan, establishing a new design principle for economically transformative nanofluids.

ideas.title: Thermoacoustic Refrigeration for Waste Heat-Driven Cooling
ideas.description: This idea expands the original inverse ORC concept by drawing an analogy from thermoacoustic refrigeration, where waste heat generates acoustic power to drive a cooling cycle. The analogous solution uses a resonant acoustic field and a stack to create a temperature gradient for cooling without complex machinery. Adapted to the ORC context, the system operates the Organic Rankine Cycle in reverse, using low-grade waste heat to power the cycle's components. This creates a precise, scalable cooling output for industrial processes, where the cooling power and temperature are controlled by modulating the working fluid properties and the cycle's pressure ratios, analogous to tuning an acoustic resonator.

ideas.title: Characterize BNTs with scanning tunneling microscopy using protein crystallography-inspired defect mapping
ideas.description: This research adapts the systematic defect-mapping methodology from protein crystallography to the characterization of boron nitride nanotubes (BNTs) using scanning tunneling microscopy (STM). In structural biology, researchers routinely compare experimental electron density maps against ideal atomic models to identify localized structural defects in proteins. Similarly, this project will use STM to acquire high-resolution spatial maps of the local density of states along synthesized BNTs. By systematically comparing these experimental electronic structure maps against computational models of ideal, defect-free BNTs, the method will enable the identification, localization, and classification of structural defects—such as boron or nitrogen vacancies, dopant atoms, or edge irregularities—that critically influence the nanotubes' electronic and mechanical properties. This goes beyond simple verification to provide a detailed defect census essential for quality control and structure-property correlation.

ideas.title: Reinforcement Learning for Real-Time Control of Nanotube Synthesis from Non-Ideal Precursors
ideas.description: This research applies reinforcement learning (RL) to autonomously control the synthesis process of boron nanotubes formed from strained or reconstructed sheets, moving beyond the ideal rolling assumption. The RL agent interacts in real-time with the synthesis environment (e.g., CVD furnace parameters), using feedback from in-situ characterization to optimize conditions for forming specific, targeted non-ideal nanotube structures. This enables the discovery of synthesis pathways for metastable or kinetically trapped nanotube morphologies with unique properties that are not accessible via traditional thermodynamic routes. The system learns a dynamic control policy that adapts to the unpredictable relaxation and reconstruction dynamics of imperfect precursor sheets, fundamentally merging materials discovery with autonomous process engineering.

ideas.title: High-throughput screening of boron nanotube nanofluids for high-power battery thermal management
ideas.description: This research applies materials informatics and high-throughput computational screening, adapted from drug discovery, to design novel nanofluids using boron nanotubes (BNTs) as the nanoparticle component. The key innovation is that these nanofluids are screened and optimized specifically for use as advanced coolants in high-power lithium-ion battery thermal management systems, targeting properties like thermal conductivity, viscosity, and electrochemical stability. The most promising candidates from the virtual screening are then synthesized and their thermal and hydraulic performance is tested not in a generic loop, but directly within a practical battery test cell, evaluating their cooling efficacy and impact on battery cycle life and power output under realistic operating conditions.

ideas.title: Availability- and Performance-Optimized ORC Systems for Real-World Building Integration
ideas.description: This research pursues system and component designs that maximize the net financial return of small-scale building ORCs by optimizing for lifetime cost-of-energy, not just upfront capital cost. It directly addresses the critical failure modes of poor reliability and variable-source performance by developing robust, low-maintenance components (e.g., hermetic expanders, fouling-resistant exchangers) and adaptive control algorithms. The design framework emphasizes modularity for easy service and integrates real-world thermal source variability and sink limitations into the optimization loop. The outcome is a system specification that guarantees high operational availability and effective energy conversion under non-ideal conditions, making the economic case for adoption robust.

ideas.title: Boron Nanotube Membranes for Degradation-Resistant Refrigerant Separation and Sensing
ideas.description: This research investigates the use of defect-engineered and doped boron nanotubes as the active material in a multifunctional membrane for organic Rankine cycle (ORC) systems using fourth-generation refrigerants. The core hypothesis is that by inducing semiconducting behavior through controlled defects, the nanotubes can act as selective molecular sieves to separate degradation byproducts (like heavier acids or polymers) from the working fluid, while simultaneously functioning as chemiresistive sensors to detect these byproducts in real-time. This approach directly addresses the long-term degradation challenge by not just studying it, but by providing an integrated material solution for in-situ mitigation and system diagnostics. The work thus bridges advanced nanomaterial property design with practical thermal system durability.

ideas.title: Intrinsically Safe Architecture for Flammable Working Fluid ORCs: System-Level Hazard Containment and Predictive Mitigation
ideas.description: This research develops and validates a system-level Intrinsically Safe Architecture (ISA) for Organic Rankine Cycle (ORC) systems using flammable natural refrigerants in vehicles. The ISA integrates three core layers: a passive, crash-resistant containment vessel for the working fluid loop; an active, inert-gas flooding system triggered by multi-sensor leak detection; and a model-predictive controller that uses operational data to anticipate and preempt hazardous states before they occur. The system's robustness is proven through coupled physical and digital testing against severe cascading failure sequences, including high-speed impact, post-crash thermal exposure, and critical component fatigue failures, ensuring containment and hazard neutralization where discrete safety systems would be overwhelmed.

ideas.title: Inverse Design of Boron Nanotubes from Predicted Reconstructed Sheet Precursors
ideas.description: This research develops a predictive, computational framework to identify which specific reconstructed boron sheets are viable precursors for stable nanotubes, moving beyond the assumption of perfect rolling from an ideal sheet. The approach inversely designs target nanotubes by first calculating the properties of known boron sheet allotropes, then modeling their rolling mechanics to map strain and stability. This theoretical screening directly guides experimental efforts to synthesize only the most promising candidates, creating a closed-loop discovery process for boron nanotubes with tailored electronic and mechanical properties.

ideas.title: Co-Design of Primary Process for Optimal Waste Heat Spectrum
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from optical communications, where a transmitter is tuned to match a fixed receiver's optimal wavelength. Here, the fixed ORC system is analogous to the receiver, with a known thermodynamic 'responsivity' based on its working fluid and heat exchanger design. The analogous solution is to design the primary engine or industrial process (the 'transmitter') to produce waste heat that is optimally matched to this fixed ORC. The adapted idea involves co-designing the primary process—potentially through staged combustion, tailored heat recovery steam generator (HRSG) design, or dynamic load-following control—to shape the temperature, flow rate, and temporal profile of the waste heat stream. The goal is to shift this 'waste heat spectrum' to fall within the narrow band where the fixed ORC's evaporator and turbine operate at peak efficiency, thereby minimizing the overall system's waste heat loss for a given power output from the ORC.

ideas.title: In-situ CVD synthesis of boron nanotube sensor skins for real-time acoustic health monitoring of ORC expanders
ideas.description: This research fuses advanced material synthesis with condition-based monitoring by using chemical vapor deposition (CVD) to directly grow dense networks of boron nanotubes (BNTs) as a conformal 'sensor skin' on the internal or external surfaces of Organic Rankine Cycle (ORC) expanders. The BNT network acts as a highly sensitive, distributed acoustic emission and vibration sensor, leveraging techniques adapted from aerospace engine diagnostics. This enables real-time, in-situ detection of micro-cracks, bearing wear, or blade imbalances by monitoring changes in the acoustic/vibrational signature captured by the BNT skin. The direct integration of the sensing material via CVD creates a seamless, durable, and high-temperature-capable diagnostic system that is fundamentally part of the component, moving beyond externally attached sensors.

ideas.title: Physics-Informed Domain Adaptation for ORC Expander Prognostics Using Hybrid Simulation-Data Transfer
ideas.description: This research develops a robust fault-diagnosis system for Organic Rankine Cycle (ORC) expanders by moving beyond a direct transfer of aerospace methods. It introduces a physics-informed domain adaptation layer to translate ORC operational data into a normalized feature space compatible with advanced diagnostic algorithms, explicitly accounting for two-phase flow and low-RPM effects. The core model is a hybrid digital twin, initially trained on high-fidelity simulations of ORC-specific faults, and then refined using a selective transfer learning protocol applied to a small set of real-world operational data. This approach ensures the diagnostic signatures are relevant to ORC failure modes while still leveraging the advanced pattern recognition structures developed in aerospace prognostics.

ideas.title: Boron Nanotube-Enhanced Heat Exchangers for Ultra-Low-Cost Building ORC Systems
ideas.description: This research direction focuses on experimentally synthesizing boron nanotubes specifically for integration into novel, low-cost heat exchanger designs for small-scale Organic Rankine Cycle (ORC) systems. The exceptional thermal conductivity, high surface area, and chemical stability predicted for boron nanotubes could enable the development of compact, highly efficient, and corrosion-resistant evaporators and condensers. By embedding these synthesized nanotubes into polymer or composite matrices, the project aims to create lightweight, formable heat exchange surfaces that can be manufactured at low cost, directly addressing the major capital cost barrier of building-based ORC units. This material-centric optimization targets a fundamental system bottleneck, potentially enabling a new class of passive, maintenance-friendly waste heat recovery systems.

ideas.title: Topological Defect-Assisted Synthesis of Boron Nanotubes from Non-Planar Precursors
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that boron nanotubes must be conceptualized as the perfect, strain-free rolling of a flat, ideal 2D boron sheet. Drawing an analogy from soft matter physics, where complex 3D liquid crystal structures are formed by strategically introducing topological defects into a 2D director field, we propose a defect-assisted synthesis pathway. The analogous solution involves using controlled defects (disclinations) as curvature directors to relieve strain and guide assembly into target morphologies. Adapted to this context, the idea is to study nanotubes formed from intrinsically strained or reconstructed boron sheets by deliberately designing and embedding topological defects—such as non-hexagonal ring clusters or grain boundaries—into the sheet precursor. These defects would act as programmed nucleation points, guiding the sheet to fold and close into a nanotube with specific structural properties (chirality, diameter, strain profile) dictated by the defect architecture, rather than by a perfect rolling transformation. This approach could open new synthetic routes to boron nanotubes with tailored electronic and mechanical properties.

ideas.title: Apply Model-Based Reinforcement Learning with Digital Twin for ORC Control
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from autonomous vehicle control, where model-based reinforcement learning is trained in a high-fidelity simulator (a digital twin) to master complex, dynamic environments. The analogous solution involves using algorithms like Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (DDPG) within a simulated driving environment to learn robust control policies before real-world application. Adapted to ORC control, we propose developing a detailed digital twin of the ORC system that models thermodynamic states, component responses, and disturbance profiles. A model-based RL agent is then trained extensively in this risk-free simulation to optimize setpoints for pumps, valves, and the expander in real-time, maximizing efficiency or power output under highly variable heat source and sink conditions, thereby bridging the sim-to-real gap for robust, adaptive ORC operation.

ideas.title: Mobile Thermal Battery Buffers with Centralized ORC Generation for Intermittent Waste Heat
ideas.description: This system separates the intermittent heat capture challenge from the capital-intensive power generation process. Standardized, shipping-container-sized 'Thermal Battery' modules are deployed at remote sites to capture and store waste heat from temporary processes using phase-change or high-capacity sensible materials. These passive, low-maintenance buffers accumulate thermal energy over time. A separate, truck-mobile ORC power generation unit then periodically tours a circuit of these sites, plugging into the charged Thermal Batteries to generate electricity over a concentrated, high-efficiency period before moving to the next site. This model maximizes the utilization and economic return of the expensive ORC equipment while simplifying the on-site technology to robust, low-cost heat storage.

ideas.title: Modular ORC with Standardized Fluid Exchange Cartridges
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that an ORC system's working fluid is a permanent, integral component. Drawing an analogy from medical dialysis systems, which use standardized, disposable filter cartridges for safe and rapid fluid exchange, the research proposes a modular ORC architecture. The core adaptation is to encapsulate the working fluid and key vulnerable components (like the evaporator and expander) within a sealed, replaceable 'energy cartridge.' This cartridge would feature standardized quick-connect ports for the external hot and cold loops. The solution enables easy, clean, and safe mid-lifecycle fluid replacement to adapt to new regulations or superior fluids, dramatically reducing retrofit costs, operational downtime, and environmental release risks during maintenance.

ideas.title: Topology-Optimized Boron Nanotube Networks for Tunable Thermoelectric Heat Exchangers
ideas.description: This research direction applies computational topology optimization to design the macro- and micro-scale architecture of heat exchangers fabricated from boron nanotube (BNT) networks. Crucially, the optimization algorithm incorporates the tunable electronic and thermal properties of BNTs, which can be modulated from metallic to semiconducting via controlled doping or defect engineering as per Idea B. This allows the simultaneous optimization of fluid-thermal performance (from Idea A) and the material's electronic transport properties within the same structure. The result is a novel class of multi-functional heat exchangers for ORC systems that can potentially convert waste heat directly to electricity via the Seebeck effect in their own walls, or actively regulate heat transfer through applied electrical fields.

ideas.title: Mapping and engineering the electronic phase diagram of boron nanotubes for targeted semiconducting states
ideas.description: This project systematically investigates the full spectrum of electronic states—metallic, semiconducting, insulating, and magnetic—induced by defects and doping in boron nanotubes. Using high-throughput density functional theory (DFT) and machine learning, it constructs a phase diagram to identify precise synthetic pathways that reliably yield semiconducting behavior with a tunable, direct band gap. The approach validates predictions through targeted synthesis using chemical vapor deposition and in-situ doping, followed by characterization via scanning tunneling spectroscopy and transport measurements to confirm the desired, robust semiconducting properties.
