ideas.title: Adapt Topological Data Analysis from Network Science to Map Multicentre Bond Networks in Boron Nanostructures
ideas.description: This research idea proposes to extend the analysis of multicentre chemical bonds in boron-based nanomaterials by drawing an analogy from network science. The analogous problem—understanding the structure and function of complex interconnected systems like social or infrastructure networks—is solved using topological data analysis and graph theory, where systems are mapped as graphs and analyzed with metrics for centrality, clustering, and community structure. The adapted idea applies this solution to boron nanostructures by modeling them as topological networks: atoms become nodes and bonding interactions (including 2-centre and multicentre bonds) become edges in a weighted graph. By applying graph-theoretic metrics, the research will systematically quantify bonding connectivity, identify key structural motifs and critical atomic 'hubs', and establish correlations between these topological features and the material's electronic properties, reactivity, and stability, providing a novel, quantitative framework for understanding bonding in complex boron nanomaterials.

ideas.title: Dynamic Co-Optimization of Lifetime Cost and Efficiency Under Future Uncertainty
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond a simple inversion of priority to a robust, integrated framework. It treats thermodynamic efficiency and total cost not as competing objectives but as interdependent variables within a system model that incorporates forecasted economic and regulatory scenarios. The research develops a decision-support tool that identifies system designs and fluid selections which are resilient to a range of plausible futures, avoiding lock-in to solutions that are cheap today but prohibitively expensive or environmentally damaging tomorrow. The outcome is a methodology for designing systems that are economically optimal and environmentally sustainable over their full operational lifetime.

ideas.title: Design and Test Boron Nanostructure Electrodes via Multicentre Bond Engineering
ideas.description: This research direction integrates fundamental bonding analysis with applied battery testing to establish a predictive design framework. By first computationally analyzing how multicentre (e.g., 3c-2e) bonds in various boron nanostructures (nanotubes, borophene, clusters) influence electron delocalization and structural flexibility, we can identify specific bonding motifs that favor high lithium-ion conductivity and mechanical resilience. These insights will then guide the targeted synthesis of the most promising boron nanostructures, which are subsequently fabricated into electrodes and rigorously tested in lithium-ion half-cells. This closes the loop from atomic-scale bonding theory to macroscopic electrochemical performance, accelerating the discovery of superior boron-based battery materials.

ideas.title: Modular ORC Units with Plug-and-Play Thermal Interface Connectors
ideas.description: This research expands the original seed idea for modular, containerized ORC units by drawing an analogy from military field medicine's solution for rapid deployment of mobile surgical units. The analogous field is Military Field Medicine and Mobile Surgical Units, where solutions like the Forward Resuscitative Surgical System utilize standardized ISO containers with pre-integrated, quick-connect utility couplings (for power, water, etc.) to achieve operational readiness in hours. The adapted idea applies this 'plug-and-play' philosophy to the thermal interface challenge. It proposes developing modular ORC units equipped with a standardized, quick-connect thermal interface system. This system would feature robust, self-sealing fluid couplings for the hot source and cold sink circuits, along with integrated control and data ports, all following a universal color/symbol code. This innovation would drastically reduce deployment time and technical expertise required at remote sites, allowing a containerized ORC unit to be rapidly connected to diverse, temporary waste heat sources—such as diesel generator exhaust, industrial flue gas stacks, or temporary incinerators—transforming it into a truly mobile and rapidly deployable waste heat recovery asset.

ideas.title: Adapting Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD) from Carbon Nanotubes for Boron Nanotube Synthesis
ideas.description: This expanded idea proposes to solve the experimental synthesis of predicted boron nanotubes by drawing a direct analogy from the successful synthesis of carbon nanotubes. In the field of carbon nanomaterials, catalytic chemical vapor deposition (CVD) was the breakthrough technique that turned theoretical predictions into reality. The adapted research idea is to develop a boron-specific CVD process. This would involve identifying a suitable volatile boron precursor and a novel catalyst/substrate combination that can template the unique, often hexagonal or triangular, boron sheet structures into tubular forms, thereby experimentally realizing the theoretically predicted boron nanotubes.

ideas.title: Adapting Aerospace Fuel System Inerting for Flammable ORC Safety
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original idea by drawing an analogy from aerospace fuel tank safety. The analogous problem is preventing catastrophic explosions in aircraft fuel tanks, which is solved using Onboard Inert Gas Generation Systems (OBIGGS) to maintain an oxygen-deficient inert atmosphere. The adapted idea is to design and test a miniaturized, vehicle-integrated inerting system specifically for flammable working fluid ORCs. This system would incorporate a compact air separation unit (e.g., membrane or pressure swing adsorption) to generate inert gas, strategically inject it into critical zones of the ORC loop (like the expander casing and fluid reservoir), and integrate sensors to monitor oxygen levels for closed-loop control, thereby creating an inherently safer system that mitigates flammability risks in dynamic vehicular environments.

ideas.title: Adaptive Hierarchical MPC for Multi-loop ORCs using a Power Grid Dispatch Analogy
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from electrical power grid management to the control of multi-loop Organic Rankine Cycle (ORC) systems under transient waste heat conditions. The analogous problem—managing a grid with fluctuating renewable generation and load—is solved using hierarchical model predictive control (MPC), where a high-level economic dispatch coordinates slower setpoints and lower-level controllers provide fast, localized regulation. The adapted idea proposes a two-tier MPC framework: a supervisory MPC optimizes the overall system's performance by coordinating the setpoints for multiple ORC loops based on forecasts of the waste heat source, analogous to grid economic dispatch. Subsequently, each ORC loop employs a dedicated, fast local MPC to precisely track its assigned setpoints and reject high-frequency disturbances in its specific heat input, ensuring stable and efficient operation despite the transient conditions across the entire system.

ideas.title: Reinforcement Learning for Co-Design of Cost-Optimized and Adaptable Small-Scale ORC Systems
ideas.description: This research fuses cost-optimized component design with reinforcement learning (RL) for real-time control by developing a co-design framework. The RL agent, trained on a digital twin of a parametrically defined, cost-constrained ORC system, learns optimal control policies that account for the performance limitations of low-cost components. The operational data and learned policies are then fed back into the system design optimization loop, identifying which cost-saving design compromises are most viable when paired with intelligent, adaptive control. This iterative process aims to discover novel, minimally viable system architectures that would be suboptimal with conventional control but achieve high lifetime value through RL-driven operational adaptation.

ideas.title: Inverse ORC-Driven Synthesis for Stabilizing Ultra-Small Nanotubes
ideas.description: This research fuses the investigation of stability in small-diameter boron nanotubes with the controlled cooling capability of an inverse Organic Rankine Cycle (ORC). The core concept is to use the inverse ORC not for industrial process cooling, but as a precise, tunable heat sink within a chemical vapor deposition (CVD) or plasma-enhanced CVD synthesis reactor. By applying rapid, spatially controlled cooling gradients during nanotube growth, the thermodynamic pathway can be manipulated to favor the formation and 'freeze-in' of ultra-small diameter nanotubes that would otherwise collapse or be unstable at synthesis temperatures. This enables the direct experimental exploration of the inverted stability-diameter relationship and the creation of novel nanostructures with potentially unique electronic and thermal properties for advanced nanofluidic or quantum devices.

ideas.title: Synthesize and Characterize Boron Nanoribbons via a Molecular Precursor Self-Assembly Pathway
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from organic electronics—the bottom-up synthesis of graphene nanoribbons from molecular precursors—to the challenge of creating finite boron nanoribbons and clusters. The analogous field solved the problem of achieving atomic precision and structural control by using designed molecular building blocks that self-assemble on a surface. The adapted research idea is to design and synthesize molecular precursors containing boron clusters or borazine rings, which are then deposited and triggered to polymerize on a suitable substrate under controlled conditions. This bottom-up pathway aims to overcome the difficulties of top-down scaling from boron sheets, allowing for the systematic fabrication and investigation of boron nanoribbons with precisely defined dimensions, edge structures, and integrated cluster motifs, thereby directly probing the scaling dual (size-dependent) properties of these finite boron nanostructures.

ideas.title: Integrated synthesis and assembly of boron-based nanotube fibers via continuous flow reactors
ideas.description: This project addresses the fundamental synthesis bottleneck by developing a reactor that integrates boron nanotube (BNT) growth with macroscopic fiber formation in one step. The core approach uses a continuous gas-phase or aerosol-assisted process where precursor gases decompose to form BNTs that are simultaneously aligned and condensed onto a rotating spool or within a converging flow. This integrated method aims to produce the first-ever macroscopic, coherent fibers from BNTs by avoiding the insolvable intermediate steps of bulk synthesis and powder processing. The research will systematically vary reactor parameters, precursors, and substrates to optimize fiber continuity, purity, and electrical conductivity.

ideas.title: Computationally Guided Synthesis of Target-Functional Boron Nanoclusters and Nanoribbons
ideas.description: This research employs high-throughput density functional theory (DFT) calculations to screen a vast space of finite boron nanoribbons and small clusters for targeted electronic, magnetic, or catalytic properties. A multi-stage filtering protocol first identifies structures with high thermodynamic and kinetic stability, then ranks them based on specific functional metrics relevant to applications like molecular electronics, spin qubits, or ligand-free catalysis. The computational pipeline outputs a prioritized list of the most promising, synthetically accessible structures with predicted properties. This list directly guides focused experimental synthesis and characterization efforts, ensuring efficient resource use and enabling direct validation of computational property predictions against measured data.

ideas.title: Closed-loop synthesis of boron nanotubes via adaptive experimental control informed by kinetic pathway modeling
ideas.description: This project establishes an integrated computational-experimental framework to overcome the kinetic and thermodynamic hurdles preventing boron nanotube synthesis. It employs high-throughput calculations to map not just stable structures, but the kinetic barriers and precursor reaction pathways likely encountered during real-world synthesis attempts. An autonomous experimental system, equipped with in situ characterization, uses this map to actively steer reaction conditions away from amorphous boron or competing crystalline phases and toward the nanotube growth window. This adaptive, closed-loop approach transforms synthesis from a blind trial-and-error process into a targeted navigation of the complex boron phase landscape.

ideas.title: Applying Topological Insulator Edge-State Engineering to Boron Nanotubes
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that all boron nanotubes are metallic by drawing an analogy from the field of topological insulators. In topological insulators, the introduction of magnetic dopants breaks time-reversal symmetry, which gaps the conducting surface states and induces insulating behavior. We adapt this solution by proposing to introduce specific symmetry-breaking structural defects or dopants (e.g., transition metal atoms) into the boron nanotube lattice. The objective is to strategically disrupt the delocalized electron network inherent to pristine boron nanotubes, potentially localizing electrons and opening a band gap. This approach focuses on engineering the electronic structure through targeted symmetry breaking rather than wholesale compositional change, investigating if this can induce semiconducting behavior in specific regions or throughout the modified nanotube.

ideas.title: Adapt CVD precursor delivery from semiconductor thin-film growth for boron nanotube synthesis
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from semiconductor thin-film manufacturing to the synthesis of boron nanotubes via CVD. In semiconductor fabrication, precise delivery of metal-organic precursors via pulsed, temperature-controlled systems is critical for growing high-quality, uniform metal oxide films (e.g., HfO2) via CVD or ALD. The analogous solution involves specialized bubblers, direct liquid injection, and pulsed gas delivery for precursor vaporization and transport. Adapted to boron nanotube (BNT) synthesis, this approach would employ a similar apparatus to deliver a volatile boron-organic precursor (e.g., triethylborane) in a controlled, pulsed manner alongside a nitrogen or reactive gas. This adapted method aims to overcome challenges in BNT growth by providing finer control over precursor concentration and reaction kinetics at the substrate, potentially leading to more uniform nucleation, controlled nanotube diameter, and improved crystalline quality compared to conventional, continuous-flow CVD setups used for carbon nanotubes.
